title: Land flint/1v0.1-16bit-cells branch on main
description:
**Coordination task for cairn.**

Bead hash-thing-1v0.1 (16-bit tagged cell encoding) is closed, but its implementation branch `flint/1v0.1-16bit-cells` is not merged to `main`. Cairn originally flagged this on the m0p discussion as a "closed bead, unmerged branch" coordination issue. Under the hash-thing-sos reframe, this merge blocks forward progress on the rest of the 1v0.x epic — 1v0.2 (material registry) and 1v0.8 (granularity) both depend on the cell encoding being live on main.

**Scope:**
- Rebase `flint/1v0.1-16bit-cells` onto current `main`
- If conflicts exist, resolve them (likely against any changes already landed via 88d or 6gf)
- Run the full quality gate: `cargo build`, `cargo test`, `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check`
- Merge to main (or open for edward's review if the rebase is non-trivial)
- Verify the branch deletion after merge

**If the branch has rotted beyond practical rebase:** close it and file a fresh 1v0.1-reland task describing what needs to be re-implemented. Don't force through a bad rebase.

**Who:** cairn is best-positioned — already scouted it, already in coordination lane. If cairn is busy, it can fall to ember.

**Source:** hash-thing-sos reframe — see `.ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md`.

title: Player entity with smooth 3D navigation
description:
The first "outside-the-grid" entity that matters for gameplay. Player has continuous position (not grid-aligned), smooth camera, WASD movement, collision against cells, and a mutation-queue producer for "place block" / "break block" / "shoot" actions. Core to the "fun playground sandbox" identity.

**Deliverables:**
- `EntityKind::Player(PlayerState)` variant
- `struct PlayerState { yaw, pitch, mode: Creative | Whatever, held_material: MaterialId, ... }`
- Camera follows player entity (replaces current orbit camera for in-world play; orbit stays for debug)
- Keyboard/mouse input → player velocity and mutation-queue events
- Collision: player entity reads cells near its position, prevents movement into solid materials
- Block place/break generates a `WorldMutation::SetCell` pushed to the queue

**Explicitly not in scope:** inventory, crafting, RPG layers, mobs, multiplayer. Just navigation + single-material place/break. Those layers come later.

**Dependencies:** 1v0.6 (entity store), 1v0.9 / NEW-B (mutation channel), 1v0.8 / NEW-A (granularity — player needs to know what "a block" is when placing).

**Priority:** P1. Core to the "fun playground sandbox" identity.

Created under hash-thing-sos reframe. See `.ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md`.

title: Mutation channel: entity→world edit API + runtime guards
description:
Implement the bounded API entities use to edit the world under the World-Sim-Closed invariant: **`entities → world edits, never world → entity state`**.

**Deliverables:**
- `enum WorldMutation { SetCell { pos, cell }, FillRegion { bounds, cell }, Impulse { pos, kind }, ... }` — a bounded, typed set
- `struct MutationQueue { pending: Vec<WorldMutation> }` — collected from entities each tick
- `World::apply_mutations(queue)` — applies in arrival order before `world.step_ca()`
- Step-loop refactor: `apply_mutations → step_ca → entities.update → clear_queue`
- Runtime guard: `debug_assert!` that `world.step_ca()` is never called with a non-empty mutation queue (catches misuse)
- Runtime guard: `debug_assert!` that entity update does not directly call any `World::set_*` method — only queue pushes allowed (catches discipline violations)
- Unit tests covering ordering, arrival-order determinism, and guard triggering

**Why this shape:** this is the enforcement mechanism for "entities → world edits, never world → entity state." 88d's `debug_assert!(step_cache.is_empty())` is the prior art — the exact same discipline-plus-runtime-guard pattern. Load-bearing for closed-world invariant: if this boundary leaks, Hashlife memoization becomes unsafe at the rule level.

**Dependencies:** must land before or alongside 1v0.6 (entity foundations). They're tightly coupled.

**Priority:** P1. Load-bearing for the closed-world invariant.

Created under hash-thing-sos reframe. See `.ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md`.

title: Cell/block granularity parameterization (N³ cells per block)
description:
Under the hash-thing-m0p resolution, "cell" is the CA primitive (octree leaf) and "block" is a gameplay grouping of cells that share a material. Parameterize the number of cells per block as a single configurable knob; the leading candidate is 8³ cells per block, with 1×1×1 as the fallback if performance is unacceptable.

**Why this matters:** this choice affects memory layout of everything downstream — storage, step time, render time. It must be experimentally revisited, not prematurely locked.

**Deliverables:**
- `const CELLS_PER_BLOCK_LOG2: u32 = 3;` (for 8³) or similar single knob
- Octree depth adjusts so the "block level" sits at `level = world_level - CELLS_PER_BLOCK_LOG2 * 3`
- UI/gameplay layer ("player places a block") translates to a cell fill operation over the N³ region
- Rendering layer adapts: at 1×1×1, block-level uniform rendering; at 8³, room for sub-block detail (marching cubes, smooth surfaces) in a later bead
- Benchmark both configurations on the current demo world: storage, step time, render time
- Document which configurations fit which gameplay affordances

**Dependencies:** requires 1v0.1 landed on main; does NOT depend on 1v0.2 or later.

**Priority:** P1 because it affects memory layout of everything downstream.

Created under hash-thing-sos reframe. See `.ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md`.

title: Re-frame 1v0.x under World-Sim-Closed
description:
**Owner: mayor (drafts), then whoever implements.**

**Context:** hash-thing-m0p resolved. The 1v0 epic ('Material-type CA & physics') survives but needs re-framing under the World-Sim-Closed principle. Mayor's job: draft the new bead structure, get edward's approval, then update the existing beads + create new ones.

**What changes per existing bead:**
- 1v0.1 (16-bit tagged cells): keeps as-is. Cell encoding is fine. BUT note: branch flint/1v0.1-16bit-cells is closed in bd but not merged to main — that's a separate landing issue cairn flagged.
- 1v0.2 (material registry): re-framed. Each material entry must carry a CaRule implementation (or fingerprint) — reactions are dispatched through the rule, not a separate interaction table.
- 1v0.3 (reaction phase): substantially reframed. Title and description change from 'hand-authored interaction table' to 'per-material CaRule registration + runtime dispatch.' Reactions aren't a phase — they're what the CA does.
- 1v0.4 (Margolus 2x2x2): keeps. Margolus becomes a reusable CaRule template, not a global phase.

**New structural beads likely needed (mayor drafts, edward approves):**
- Cell/block granularity parameterization (N³ cells per block; start with 8³; fallback to 1×1×1)
- Entity system (outside-the-grid data structure with mutation-queue producers)
- Mutation channel (contract between entities and world — the bounded API for entity→world edits)
- Plugin API epic (materials, entities, rules, renderers, gameplay modes — subject to the plugin survey hash-thing-ore)
- Multiplayer model exploration task (lockstep vs alternatives)

**Source of truth:** .ship-notes/metaverse-design-decision.md (RESOLVED section).

**Deliverable:** mayor writes a proposal in .ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md with the new bead structure (titles, descriptions, dependencies). Edward reviews before any bead is created or updated. Mayor does NOT close/edit existing 1v0.x beads until the proposal is approved.

**Not blocking crew:** this is a meta task. Other lanes (88d merge cleanup, 5bb SVDAG work, 3fq terrain gen) should proceed independently. Only 1v0.x is frozen pending reframe.
close_reason: Reframe draft applied autonomously per edward's 'don't wait' instruction. 11 bead edits/creates, see closing comment.

title: Metaverse vision — substrate vs game-engine decision
description:
Edward dropped ONE_POSSIBLE_METAVERSE_VISION.md (committed at main@910d16c). Full triage in that file + AGENTS.md peer-autonomy section. Core tension: is hash-thing a 3D CA engine that grows a game layer (substrate-first) or a voxel game engine that supports CAs as one rule type (game-first)? This is a one-way door. Both are good; picking determines the next 6 months.

Substrate-first implications:
- CellState stays u8 (or small enum)
- Mods = alternate CaRule impls
- Hashlife step caching is the point
- Modding API is Rust plugin traits
- No networking until a compelling reason

Game-first implications:
- CellState becomes typed block+behavior enum
- Mods = scripted block behaviors with world hooks
- Step caching is an optimization for one use case
- Lua/scripting is in-scope
- Networking / server hosting is in-scope eventually

Compatible-to-absorb-now regardless of pick:
- Infinite vertical (octree already supports)
- Handcrafted vanilla starting worlds (extend seed generation)
- Rust modding for CaRule (trait-based plugin system)

Park (north-star, either path):
- Multiplayer / portals / server hosting
- Cosmetic block graphics, skins
- Server-sends-mods (client/server split)
- ML block-structure generation

For mayor: this is a design-ledger entry, not a sub-dividable backlog. File it, summarize for edward, do not block flowing work on its resolution. Edward resolves by picking substrate-first or game-first. Everything else rides on that call.

title: SVDAG 31-bit node-count overflow silently corrupts as leaf
description:
**Discovered by:** Gemini in the Trident review of d7c5b4d (hash-thing-1v0.1, 16-bit cells).

## Problem

`src/render/svdag.rs` uses the high bit of a u32 as `LEAF_BIT` (0x8000_0000) to distinguish interior child slots from leaf slots. Interior slots store `nodes.len() as u32` as the child offset.

If the flattened node array ever grows to `>= 2^31 = ~2.1 billion u32 slots (~8.6 GB)`, a legitimate interior index will have bit 31 set and get misread as a leaf with an absurd material. At ~36 bytes per interior node (9 u32s), that's ~238 million nodes — plausibly reachable once world size scales past 1024³.

This is a silent overflow, not an error. The GPU will decode a random interior offset as a leaf and produce garbage material / corrupted frames.

## Reproduction path

Not yet empirically reproduced. Trigger conditions:
- Node array exceeds ~2^31 u32 slots (~8.6 GB on GPU), OR
- Any write to `nodes[header_start + 1 + i] = child_offset` where `child_offset >= 2^31`.

## Fix options

1. **Assert at write time.** Cheapest: `assert!(child_offset < LEAF_BIT, "SVDAG interior count exceeded 31 bits")` in `write_node` before the slot assignment. Fails loudly instead of corrupting.
2. **Widen the encoding.** Use a u64 node buffer or split mask/children into separate buffers; removes the 31-bit cap entirely.
3. **Out-of-band leaf table.** Keep interiors as u32 offsets (full range), put leaf materials in a separate buffer referenced by a reserved sentinel.

Option 1 is the right immediate fix (preserves current layout, stops silent corruption). Options 2/3 are follow-up work when 2^31 becomes a real pressure.

## Scope

- `src/render/svdag.rs:84` (`nodes.len() as u32`)
- `src/render/svdag.rs:104,111,114` (child slot writes)
- Matching GPU decode in `src/render/svdag_raycast.wgsl`

## Notes

Filed as a discovered-from dependency of hash-thing-1v0.1. Unblocks once hash-thing-1v0.1 merges to main (which already has the `Cell` invariant enforced in release).

title: bytes_per_row hardcoded to volume_size breaks on format widening (u8→u16)
description:
Code review important I7 (Claude Standard + Critical).

src/render/renderer.rs:371 — bytes_per_row: Some(self.volume_size) sets 64 bytes at VOLUME_SIZE=64. This is technically correct for R8Uint (1 byte/texel) but:

(a) Not a multiple of wgpu's COPY_BYTES_PER_ROW_ALIGNMENT = 256. wgpu allows unaligned write_texture only if the copy is a single row; a 64^3 copy has 4096 rows. Backend-dependent whether this is validated or silently uploads garbage.

(b) Wrong semantically the moment the format widens to R16Uint for 16-bit cells (hash-thing-1v0.1). Will upload half the data or crash on validation.

FIX: Compute bytes_per_row = volume_size * bytes_per_texel, and pad to COPY_BYTES_PER_ROW_ALIGNMENT with a staging row if needed. Extract a helper so the computation lives in one place.

Blocks hash-thing-1v0.1 (CellState u8→u16 widening). Fix should land together with 1v0.1 or immediately before.

title: SVDAG shader EPS step doesn't guarantee forward progress at deep levels
description:
Code review blocker B5 (Claude Critical, novel finding — sibling of the already-fixed EPS-pop bug).

src/render/svdag_raycast.wgsl:116,193 and src/render/svdag.rs:214 (CPU replica) — after step-past, we do 't += 1e-5'. The pop-check comment (added in the recent fix) asserts '|rd_axis| * EPS > 0' is the safety margin. But at MAX_DEPTH=14 one voxel is 1/16384 ≈ 6.1e-5, and for a near-grazing ray (|rd_axis| ≈ 0.01) the pos advance is 1e-7 — two orders below the voxel. The ray re-enters the same octant, oct_hit.y is the same, t increments by exactly 1e-5 again. It stalls until MAX_STEPS fires (B4).

The strict-pop fix cured one failure mode (infinite loop on boundary crossing) but left the other (no forward progress when |rd_axis| small) untouched.

TRIGGER:
- camera_dist=0.5 (zoom in so rays graze)
- VOLUME_SIZE pushed toward MAX_DEPTH
- near-axis-aligned camera direction
Visible symptom: persistent dead zone / missing pixels at glancing angles.

FIX: compute step in terms of slab size.
- Option A (t-space): 't += max(1e-5, oct_hit.y * 1e-6)'
- Option B (world-space): track pos directly, nudge by 'max(slab_min / 1024, 1e-6)' along rd

Standard Laine-Karras 'ray parameter slack' formulation is Option B.

Add a grazing-ray test in src/render/svdag.rs cpu_trace::tests targeting deep levels before fixing.

title: SVDAG MAX_STEPS=512 guarantees false misses at 4096+ scale
description:
Code review blocker B4 (Codex Standard + Critical, novel finding).

src/render/svdag_raycast.wgsl:81,118-196 and src/render/svdag.rs:142-145,214-314 — both shader and CPU replica hard-cap traversal at MAX_STEPS=512. Each loop advances one octant exit, so step count grows with ray path length, not frame size.

SPEC target is 4096^3+ (SPEC.md:72,166-190). A ray crossing a deeply subdivided sparse scene can require far more than 512 empty-octant exits before termination. When that happens, traversal silently returns miss/background instead of failing loudly.

This is the worst failure mode for a renderer: 'looks fine' until distant or thin geometry just disappears.

FIX:
- Derive the step budget from root_level (rough upper bound ~3 * 2^root_level for straight-line traversal, tighter for realistic rays).
- OR remove the fixed cap from the correctness path and guarantee progress by boundary crossing rather than a demo-sized loop budget.
- Fail loudly (flag pixel) when the budget is exhausted so misses are debuggable.
- Add a worst-case sparse-path test that proves the shader/CPU pair still hit or exit correctly above 512 octant transitions.

Must land before pushing volume size above 256.

title: Out-of-bounds coordinates silently alias to border voxel in set_cell/get_cell
description:
Code review blocker B3 (Codex Standard + Critical, novel finding).

src/octree/store.rs:103-137 and src/sim/world.rs:28-35 — set_cell and get_cell recurse by comparing '>= half' and subtracting half per level. They never verify the original coordinate is < side. Any coordinate beyond the root extent keeps taking the '+' octant branch until it lands on the outermost voxel.

For a 64-wide world, set(64, 0, 0) walks 'upper octant' at every level and aliases to the last in-bounds leaf (63, 0, 0) instead of being rejected. Silent data corruption.

Trigger: any set/get call with coord outside [0, side). Today the public API makes it easy and nothing defends against it.

FIX: bounds check at the World API boundary. Short term, Result/Option or debug_assert! on out-of-range. Longer term, route out-of-range writes through explicit root expansion (SPEC.md:72,76 lazy root growth).

Must land before any terrain-gen, chunk-stitching, or edit-tool work touches edge coordinates.

title: Hash-cons store is append-only — leaks every dead generation forever
description:
Code review blocker B2 (Codex Critical, novel finding).

src/octree/store.rs:35-42 — NodeStore::intern_node only appends to nodes and intern. There is no refcount, no reachability pass, no epoch compaction. World::step_flat (src/sim/world.rs:45-61) rebuilds a fresh root every tick into the same store, so every obsolete generation's subtrees are retained.

Impact: memory use, hash lookup cost, and SVDAG rebuild cost all trend upward with runtime, not with live-scene size. store.stats() reports 'all states ever seen' not current scene, which poisons any future scaling analysis.

SHORT-TERM FIX: rebuild each generation into a fresh NodeStore, or add reachability-based compaction / refcounting.

LONG-TERM: This is entangled with the Stepper-trait refactor. Once step_cache moves out of NodeStore into per-Stepper caches, the arena can be designed with explicit eviction policy from day one. Worth considering whether to do this as a standalone fix or fold into the Stepper refactor.

If historical interning is intentional, document it as a bounded cache with an eviction story. Right now it is unbounded.

title: set_cell silently corrupts trees when Leaf appears at intermediate level
description:
Code review blocker B1 (Claude Critical, novel finding).

src/octree/store.rs:103-122 — set_cell's recursive descent matches Node::Leaf(_) and unconditionally returns self.leaf(state), regardless of whether we're at level 0. For any level>0 node whose octant child happens to be a Leaf, this replaces the entire 2^n-sized subtree with a single leaf cell.

DORMANCY: Today World::new uses uniform(level, 0) which interns Interior-all-the-way-down, so the Leaf arm never fires at an intermediate level. The bug detonates the moment any caller interns a Leaf at level>0 — uniform-subtree compression, Hashlife empty-subtree collapse, or anyone calling interior([leaf, leaf, ...]).

FIX: Match on level inside the Leaf arm. If target_level > 0, expand Leaf → Interior-of-8-identical-leaves at the current level, then recurse. The correct idiom is:
  Leaf(s) if target_level > 0 => expand-and-recurse

Must land before the Stepper-trait / Hashlife refactor or canonicalization work.

title: SVDAG shader renders sparse scenes sparsely (bug)
description: SVDAG render path correctly draws dense regions (16^3 cube works) but only renders a fraction of filled voxels in sparse scenes. Test 1: 2488-cell random sphere shows only ~10-15 scattered voxels instead of dense surface. Test 2: 8 corner clusters (4^3 each, 512 voxels total) only 2 of 8 clusters visible. Hash-conses down to 6 unique nodes so serialization is correct. Needs investigation of the iterative descent/pop/step logic in svdag_raycast.wgsl — suspect step-past-empty octant or pop-until-contains logic skips filled sibling octants in some cases. Flat 3D path renders all three test cases correctly.

title: Serialize hash-consed DAG to GPU buffers
description: Contiguous layout for interior nodes (child pointers + occupancy mask) and leaf attributes. Upload as storage buffers. HashDAG-style so edits can be diff-uploaded.

title: Iterative SVDAG traversal shader
description: Laine-Karras lineage: iterative descent with position tracking. Start correctness-first, optimize later. Matches existing raycaster camera/orbit controls.

title: Entity system foundations (particles as first kind)
description:
First implementation of the **entity store** — a data structure living alongside the octree, holding out-of-grid things with continuous positions. Particles (sparks, smoke, thrown objects) are the first entity kind because they're the simplest: short-lived, no AI, no inventory, just position + velocity + render hint.

**Mayor correctly flagged this as a design gate** (capability shift, new coupling protocol). That's right — this IS the entity system, not a sidecar feature.

**Entity store properties under World-Sim-Closed:**
- Entities hold read-only references to the octree (they can *look* at cells near them for spawning, despawning, rendering hints)
- Entities never directly mutate the octree — they push `WorldMutation`s onto the mutation channel (see NEW-B), applied at tick boundaries by `apply_mutations(world, queue)` before `world.step_ca()`
- Entities have their own update phase after world step: `entities.update(world)` can read cells and update entity positions/velocities
- Step loop becomes: `apply_mutations → step_ca → entities.update → clear_queue`

**Deliverables:**
- `EntityId(u64)`, `struct Entity { pos: Vec3, vel: Vec3, kind: EntityKind, state: EntityState }`
- `EntityKind::Particle(...)` as the first variant
- `EntityStore` with add / remove / iterate
- Step loop refactor wiring `apply_mutations → step_ca → entities.update`
- Renderer hook for particles (separate pass from voxel rendering)

Future entity kinds (`Player` via NEW-C, `Mob`, `Item`, `Projectile`) reuse this foundation.

**Dependencies (new under sos):** NEW-B (mutation channel) must land before or alongside this. See `.ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md`.

title: Material registry with per-material rules
description:
Registry keyed by material ID. Each entry contains:

- **Visual properties** (color / texture ref) — CPU + GPU, for rendering
- **Physical properties** (density, flammability, conductivity, ...) — CPU, for rule evaluation and UI
- **Rule ID** — index into a parallel `Vec<Box<dyn CaRule>>` that defines how cells of this material step each tick

Start with room for 256 materials, design for >256. Rule IDs may point at a shared rule (e.g., all "static decorative blocks" share `NoopRule`) or a unique per-material rule (e.g., `FireRule`).

**Reframe under hash-thing-sos:** material entries carry a rule pointer, not just properties. Reactions are dispatched per-cell by looking up the center material's rule and invoking it. Hand-authored interaction tables become per-rule match statements inside individual `CaRule` impls — no central interaction table. See `.ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md`.

title: Per-material CaRule dispatch in World::step
description:
Replace the reaction-table concept with **per-material `CaRule` dispatch**. `World::step` iterates cells; for each cell, looks up the center material's rule in the registry and invokes `rule.step_cell(center, neighbors)`. Each material's rule handles its own reactions (e.g., `FireRule` checks neighbors for wood/leaves and propagates; `WaterRule` checks neighbors for lava and produces stone). **No central interaction table.**

**Deliverables:**
- `trait CaRule { fn step_cell(&self, center: Cell, neighbors: &[Cell; 26]) -> Cell; }` — pure, no context, no RNG, no tick number, no entity state
- `NoopRule` for static materials (stone, dirt)
- `FireRule`, `WaterRule` as the first non-trivial examples
- Dispatch loop in `World::step` reads each cell's material and calls the matching rule
- Integration with existing brute-force stepping; Hashlife integration comes later in 6gf

**Why this shape:**
1. Central interaction tables force all reactions through one choke point, which accidentally becomes the "pure rule" boundary. Per-material rules give each material explicit ownership of its own physics.
2. Modding: adding a new reactive material = writing one `CaRule`, not editing a global table. Plugin-friendly.
3. Hashlife memoization: each rule has a fingerprint; `(subtree, rule_set_fingerprint, generations)` is a clean memo key.
4. 1v0.4 and 1v0.5 become users of this trait, not competitors to it.

**Purity enforcement:** discipline + runtime guards (not structural). `CaRule` trait is purely documented as pure; lint rules + code review catch violations. Prior art: 88d's `debug_assert!(step_cache.is_empty())`. See `.ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md`.

title: Margolus 2x2x2 movement phase
description:
Partition world into non-overlapping 2x2x2 blocks, apply permutation rules per block, alternate partition offset each generation. Mass-conserving because you rearrange rather than create/destroy.

**Reframe under hash-thing-sos:** Margolus is implemented as a **reusable `CaRule` template** rather than a separate phase. A `MargolusRule<F>` type parameterized on a block-transformation function; materials that want Margolus-style movement register an instance. This way gravity (1v0.5), sand flow, lava flow, etc. all reuse the same template with different parameters. Still bit-preserving, still non-overlapping, still alternating offsets — just invoked through the `CaRule` dispatch from 1v0.3 instead of a sidecar phase. See `.ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md`.

title: 16-bit tagged cell encoding
description: Pack material-ID + metadata into 16 bits (expand to 32 if we hit the wall). Tagged-pointer style — Hashlife doesn't care about the split, it just memoizes state patterns.

title: Recursive Hashlife step on octree
description: Implement recursive step: at each level, combine 8 child results into the next NodeId. Start correctness-first using the existing brute-force stepper as reference. Direct octree operation, no flatten-to-grid.

title: Memoize step results by (NodeId, phase)
description: Add a step-result cache in NodeStore keyed on (NodeId, phase_or_rule). This is the core Hashlife performance win — identical subtrees compute their step exactly once.

title: Material-type CA & physics
description:
Closed-world material system: 16-bit tagged cells + material registry with per-material CaRules + Margolus rule template for movement + entity system foundations (starting with particles). Implements the "World Sim is Closed" principle from `.ship-notes/metaverse-design-decision.md` — rules are pure functions of their neighborhood; entities interact with the world only through a bounded mutation channel.

**Key invariant:** `entities → world edits, never world → entity state`. Rules are pure `(center_cell, &neighbors[26]) -> cell`. No tick number, no RNG, no global state, no entity state readable inside rules. This keeps Hashlife memoization structurally valid regardless of gameplay richness.

**Children (post-sos reframe):**
- 1v0.1 16-bit cells (closed, needs branch merge)
- 1v0.2 material registry with per-material rules
- 1v0.3 per-material CaRule dispatch in World::step
- 1v0.4 Margolus 2×2×2 as reusable CaRule template
- 1v0.5 gravity and fluid flow via Margolus rules
- 1v0.6 entity system foundations (particles as first kind)
- 1v0.7 demo scene
- NEW-A cell/block granularity parameterization (N³ cells per block)
- NEW-B mutation channel spec
- NEW-C player entity with smooth 3D navigation

See `.ship-notes/reframe-1v0.md` for per-bead transformation details.

title: SVDAG rendering
description: HashDAG-style SVDAG raycaster to replace the flat 3D-texture path at 1024^3+. Iterative GPU traversal (Laine-Karras lineage) with per-leaf material attributes and incremental edit uploads.

title: Hashlife octree stepping
description: Recursive Hashlife-style step on the hash-consed octree, replacing the temporary brute-force grid stepper. Memoize step results per (NodeId, phase). This is load-bearing for all simulation performance.

title: 52b Phase C: tighten bd claim semantics — prevent branch-independent closures
description: Root-cause fix for the hash-thing-52b pattern: two seats independently claim+close the same bead on disconnected branches. Phase B (merge-to-main-directly) reduces the window but doesn't eliminate it. Options: (1) bd close --require-commit <sha> that refuses close if sha not in current branch ancestry; (2) server-side hook on push that rejects if close-event refers to a commit not reachable from origin/main at push time; (3) claim-ownership audit trail that reveals branch context. Pick approach + implement.

title: Create mayor skill — migrate all mayor process out of project CLAUDE.md
description:
Edward wants all mayor process to live in a skill, not in project `CLAUDE.md`. Project `CLAUDE.md` stays terse and agent-agnostic; the skill holds mayor-specific process that non-mayor workers don't need to read.

## Scope — what moves into the skill

- **Starter queue logic** — `bd list --status blocked` as first action, plus design-summary writing per bead, plus the "do the invocation task, then park" rule.
- **`claude-md-edit` queue processing** — wake triggers, processing steps, batching, surfacing to edward, closing on ack, stale-bead nudge policy.
- **Pre-flight checklist** — every mayor session startup sequence (drain `claude-md-edit` queue → refresh ledger → handle explicit invocation).
- **On-demand-only rule** — mayor does not sweep for background work; do the task you were invoked for, then park.
- **Wake-trigger mechanism** — how workers spawn mayor background sessions, cadence tuning.

Currently most of this is scattered between "The mayor" section in project `CLAUDE.md`, session memory, and mayor-session chat. Consolidate.

## Skill location — open question for the implementer

Three candidates (mayor push-up offered the decision):

1. **User-global** `~/.claude/skills/mayor/` — shared across all edward's projects. Problem: hash-thing's crew names (flint, cairn, onyx, etc.) are project-specific; global skill would have to parameterize them.

title: Add commit step to /ship skill workflow
description:
Edward's ask: add an explicit commit step to the `/ship` skill workflow.

## Why

Today `/ship` produces monolithic end-of-run commits. A single `/ship` invocation for a feature often lands as one or two commits — too coarse for granular history, bisectability, and the "commit at natural boundaries" rule being landed in project `CLAUDE.md` alongside this bead.

## Desired behavior

The `/ship` skill phases should explicitly commit at natural boundaries, e.g.:
- After plan file written (`plan-<actor>-<id>.md`)
- After first implementation milestone (first green test)

- At push/landing

Each phase produces a commit, not just the final landing step.

## Open questions for the implementer

## Scope

title: Determinism audit: catalog and eliminate non-determinism in sim path
description:
**Standing task.** Catalog and eliminate non-determinism sources in the simulation path — `src/sim/`, `src/octree/`, and any code inside the tick loop.

**Why this is useful NOW, not just later:**
1. **Hashlife memoization.** Non-deterministic rules poison the memo cache in 6gf. If `CaRule::step_cell` ever reads external state, the memoization assumption breaks silently.
2. **World-Sim-Closed enforcement.** The discipline this audit tracks IS the enforcement of the closed-world invariant.
3. **Multiplayer readiness.** When hash-thing-3bj (lockstep v1) becomes real work, the audit is already done. Zero-surprise deployment.

**Scope (from `.ship-notes/multiplayer-model-options.md` Section on Rust footguns):**
- `std::collections::HashMap` / `HashSet` — randomized hasher → iteration order varies per process. **Fix:** replace with `BTreeMap`/`BTreeSet` or `FxHashMap` with audited insertion order. Audit every HashMap in `src/sim/` and `src/octree/`.
- `f32` / `f64` in the CA pipeline — RFC 3514 formalizes that Rust float ops are not bit-exact across targets. **Fix:** keep CA integer (hash-thing already does). Audit that floats never feed the mutation channel or the CA step.
- `rand::thread_rng()` seeded from OS — non-deterministic across runs. **Fix:** use `SmallRng::seed_from_u64(tick)` or similar. Audit all `rand` uses in sim code.
- `rayon` parallel reductions on floats — order-dependent. **Fix:** integer sums, explicit sequential reduction, or deterministic parallel combiner.
- `std::time::Instant` / wall clock leaking into sim logic. **Fix:** sim code should never read `Instant`.
- `#[derive(Hash)]` on structs containing floats. **Fix:** don't.
- Allocator address leakage (`*const T as usize` as hash input). **Fix:** never.

**Deliverables:**
- Audit spreadsheet (or markdown table) in `.ship-notes/determinism-audit.md` listing every offender, severity, and fix status
- Fix PRs for each identified issue
- A `deny(clippy::...)` lint configuration or custom lint that flags regressions
- Ideally: a `DeterminismGuard` newtype that wraps the CA step phase with `debug_assert!`s

**Ownership:** unclaimed, P2. First seat to claim it owns the discipline. Expected to take ~1 week of focused audit + fix PRs.

**Current known offenders:** TODO — populated as audit progresses.

**Source:** `.ship-notes/multiplayer-model-options.md` (Rust-specific determinism checklist section).

title: Multiplayer v1: deterministic lockstep implementation
description:
**Placeholder epic for v1 multiplayer implementation — deferred until single-player 1v0.x is substantially fun.**

**Architecture:** deterministic lockstep (Factorio model). See `.ship-notes/multiplayer-model-options.md` for full survey and rationale.

**Why lockstep:** hash-thing's architecture already pays for the hardest parts — mutation channel (1v0.9) IS the wire format; hash-consed root NodeId IS the desync-detection Merkle hash; step loop IS the lockstep frame; Hashlife memoization already requires deterministic rules. Lockstep costs approximately zero additional effort beyond what the closed-world invariant requires.

**Sketch scope (from the options doc "Concrete v1 implementation plan"):**
1. Determinism audit of existing sim code (~1 week, but useful for Hashlife regardless)
2. `DeterminismGuard` newtype wrapping the CA step phase with `debug_assert!`s
3. `WorldMutation` serialization (bincode or postcard)
4. Input-buffered P2P mesh with stamped inputs
5. Desync detection via root-NodeId comparison every N ticks
6. Latency hiding for local block placement
7. Lobby / join flow — initial sync is trivial (ship `root: NodeId` + let new peer fetch subtree graph)

**Total estimate:** ~4 weeks of focused work once 1v0.9 (mutation channel) lands.

**Player ceiling:** 4-16 players. Lockstep's slowest-peer problem caps here.

**NOT in this epic:** rollback optimizations (see hash-thing-rollback-multiplayer), MMO scaling, anti-cheat, public-server safety.

**Dependencies:** 1v0.9 (mutation channel) and the determinism-audit bead must land first.

**Trigger to unblock:** single-player is "fun enough to want a friend in it." Not before.

title: SVDAG fuzz harness as permanent seeded property test
description: Post-27m follow-up. The 27m regression tests ship three hand-plucked tuples from a fuzz sweep (seed 0xC0FFEE_u64, 2000 rays, |rd| ~ 1e-3..3e-2, depth 12) that no longer exists in-tree. Per Claude Critical / Gemini Evolutionary / Codex Evolutionary: promote the fuzz harness to a permanent seeded property test so the 711/2000→0/2000 claim is reproducible and future regressions catch the whole distribution, not just the fossils. Shape: loop over 2000 deterministic (ro, rd) cases via the existing RNG infrastructure in svdag.rs:503-513; assert result.steps < 400 for each AND assert t_new > t_old on every StepPast event. Keep the three frozen cases as a fast smoke test. May also want to assert hit/miss outcomes per Codex Standard §1.

title: SVDAG ray-octant mirroring (Laine-Karras Stage 2)
description: Post-27m roadmap item. All three evolutionary reviewers (claude/codex/gemini) converged on this as the next step after 27m. Mirror the ray at entry: for any negative rd component, negate it and XOR the octant bit used to index children. Traversal then only ever sees componentwise-positive rd. Gains: (1) removes all abs(rd.*) in exit-axis selection and dt_cap (svdag_raycast.wgsl:205-225, svdag.rs:331-352), (2) makes exit axis a bit-identity not a float comparison, (3) removes ambiguity on simultaneous ties (partial fix for hash-thing-6hd), (4) sets up Stage 3 integer DDA. Scope: ~30 lines in both files; no DAG format changes; mirror the XOR through octant_of and the descend loop. Reference: Laine & Karras 2010 'Efficient Sparse Voxel Octrees' §3.

title: SVDAG corner-exit wrong-sibling at simultaneous tied axes
description:
Found during 27m trident review (Codex Critical Important #2). When a ray exits a child cell on two axes simultaneously (exact corner crossing), the step-past code identifies only one exit axis and applies the nudge only on that axis. The untied axis's position can end up EXACTLY on the parent midpoint. octant_of uses >= to classify — which biases boundary points to the HIGH half on every axis. For a ray exiting x+/y-, the correct next child is (x-high, y-low) but octant_of returns (x-high, y-high).

Codex reproducer (float32): rd = (1.0, -1e-6, 0.0), pos lands at (0.50000995, 0.5, 0.25) after step. octant_of returns 3 (x-high, y-high) but physically correct is 1 (x-high, y-low).

Fix direction: either (a) nudge all tied axes, (b) after step, compute octant using sign(rd) on tied axes rather than pos, or (c) clamp tied-axis pos to the correct half based on rd sign.

Not yet observed as a visible rendering artifact. Latent issue with adversarial rays at exact ties. Scope-controlled out of 27m to keep that fix narrow.

title: Worktree→main merge process undefined; dual h34.3 implementations need reconciliation
description:
Scout find from autopilot session 2026-04-11: the multi-worktree development model has no defined 'land to main' process, and this has produced divergent parallel implementations of the same beads. The immediate symptom is two independent h34.3 implementations that claim to close the same bead.

## The symptom

```
$ git log --oneline origin/main..origin/worktree-vast-leaping-allen | head -3
f0a7669 docs(SPEC): h34 3/4 — include determinism audit landing
9981d7d ci(k5r): warn-only cargo fmt --check gate on ci.yml
d82986b chore(fmt): clean main.rs + rng.rs cargo fmt drift
... (17 commits total)

$ git log --oneline origin/main..origin/worktree-sleepy-wiggling-fountain
0abe192 docs: refresh SPEC.md current state after foundations + terrain landings
30fb21f Add terrain-gen perf tracking (hash-thing-3fq.5)
462d764 Add perf measurement infrastructure (hash-thing-h34.3)   ← !
6b21292 chore: determinism audit + seed_center fix (hash-thing-h34.2)
a171330 fix: address review findings on terrain generator
9d21d73 Add procedural terrain generator (hash-thing-3fq.1)
```

Both worktrees are 6-17 commits ahead of origin/main. Neither has been merged.

## The two h34.3 implementations

### vast-leaping-allen (1178249)

    src/perf.rs: Ring<Duration> of 64 samples, mean + p95 aggregation,
    HashMap<&'static str, Ring>, Perf::time(name, closure) convenience,
    consolidated per-generation log line with step_cpu / upload_cpu /
    render_cpu metrics.

API surface: `Perf::record(&mut self, name, Duration)`, `Perf::time(name, closure)`, `Perf::clear()`, `Perf::summary()`.

### sleepy-wiggling-fountain (462d764)

    src/perf.rs: PerfCounters POD struct tracking per-op samples for
    step / gen / svdag with last, average, peak, plus ratcheting peak on
    NodeStore node count and step-cache size. memory_bytes_estimate()
    uses compile-time size_of::<Node>() + NodeId.

API surface: different struct name, different field set, tracks different metrics (node-count ratchet is absent from vast's version), uses a different time scope helper.

**These do not reconcile via rebase.** They are conceptually-overlapping but mechanically-disjoint implementations of the same spec bullet. Picking one means discarding the other.

## The dual h34.2 situation

Related symptom: hash-thing-h34.2 (determinism audit) is marked closed in bd (which is branch-agnostic shared state via .beads/issues.jsonl), but the **commit** that actually contains the audit (6b21292 'chore: determinism audit + seed_center fix') lives on sleepy-wiggling-fountain only. vast-leaping-allen has no audit commit.

I committed SPEC.md refresh on vast-leaping-allen (f0a7669) citing h34.2 as landed — citing work that is on a *different branch* than the one my commit lives on. From the perspective of anyone reading origin/main, that SPEC.md refresh is ahead of its referenced commit.

## Why this is P2, not a nit

This is not a process-aesthetics issue. It's load-bearing for multiple ongoing claims:

1. **Stale bead assignments.** Both worktrees claim → close h34.3 without seeing each other's work. The crew's lane discipline assumes `bd update <id> --claim` is atomic, but atomicity only protects against *simultaneous* claims — it does not catch 'two claims separated by hours, resolved independently'.

2. **My current session's commits are poisoned.** dd712b2 (main.rs NLL borrow cleanup) calls `self.perf.record('render_cpu', ...)` — that exact API is specific to vast-leaping-allen's Perf type. If sleepy's PerfCounters lands on main first, dd712b2 rebases into a compile error.

3. **SPEC.md drift.** The current SPEC.md on each branch describes the work in that branch. There is no single source of truth. Future sessions reading origin/main's SPEC.md will see a pre-h34.3 narrative; future sessions reading either worktree will see different post-h34.3 narratives.

4. **Scout decisions compound the drift.** I just filed hash-thing-ca0 (test coverage for octree/node.rs), hash-thing-1di (test coverage for sim/rule.rs), hash-thing-nna (test coverage for sim/world.rs) — all against the line numbers and API of vast-leaping-allen's code. sleepy-wiggling-fountain has the same files at different content (e.g. world.rs has terrain-gen integration from 3fq.1). The test coverage beads will have subtly wrong line refs for whichever branch lands second.

## What this bead ships

### Phase A: immediate decision on h34.3

One of vast's or sleepy's h34.3 implementations wins; the other becomes a cross-branch rebase exercise. Candidates for choosing:

- Mean/p95 (vast) is the state-of-the-art single-number summary; node-count ratcheting (sleepy) is long-tail memory watchdog. Both are useful. The honest move is to merge them: take vast's mean/p95 Ring API and add sleepy's NodeStore node-count ratchet as an additional metric family.
- Whoever picks this up: do the merge on a third branch (say, `cairn/h34.3-reconcile`), and close **both** 462d764 and 1178249 via rebase-onto.

### Phase B: define the branch structure

Either:

1. **Each worktree is a disposable feature branch.** Commits are landed via PR to main, worktree is deleted once merged. `main` is always authoritative. Requires tooling: a `/land` slash command that opens a PR against main and waits for merge.

2. **One worktree is the staging branch, others are feature branches.** Some branch is designated the integration point, PRs target it, and it merges to main weekly/on-demand.

3. **Direct pushes to main.** Every session that lands a commit pushes to main. Worktree branches are pre-push staging only. Requires merge conflict resolution during push; crew discipline to keep main green.

Pick one. Document in AGENTS.md. Today, the unwritten process appears to be 'push to a worktree branch and hope'. That's how we got the dual h34.3.

### Phase C: tighten bd claim semantics

`bd update <id> --claim` only checks 'is someone currently assigned'. It does not check 'does an existing commit on any branch already close this bead'. The fix: when closing a bead, require the commit SHA to be on origin/main, not just on any branch. Optional: warn on claim if bd has seen a prior closure for this bead from a different branch.

## Non-goals for this bead

- Do not rewrite the two h34.3 implementations. Pick one or merge, don't start over.
- Do not delete either worktree branch until Phase A lands — both contain work that needs to migrate.
- Do not retroactively reassign beads between worktrees. Just reconcile the code.

## Lane

Process / infra. Ember or cairn, depending on who has bandwidth. Edward can also drive directly since Phase B is a gate-tier process call (how should we land code?), and I'm filing this in autopilot mode which would normally park it at that gate.

**Autopilot action:** parked at Phase B for edward's next touch (how should worktree branches land?). Phase A (reconcile h34.3) is autonomous once Phase B's structure is decided.

Filed by ember from autopilot scout ladder step 3 (improve crew infra).
close_reason: Phase A: vast's 1178249 Perf picked (Ring+p95+generic, covers render loop, bounded memory, scales with new ops). Phase B: merge-to-main policy documented in CLAUDE.md + actually landed this session (vast→main at eb44263). Phase C split out to hash-thing-s89. Follow-ups yb5 (memory watchdog), q63 (wall-clock cadence) filed.

title: Plugin surface design survey
description:
Research/exploration task. Owner: flint (per edward).

title: Add cargo fmt --check gate to ci.yml (warn-only → hard-fail)
description:
xb7.1 CI gates cargo check + actionlint + (warn-only) clippy but does NOT gate cargo fmt. Result: fmt drift has silently accumulated across 5 files over recent commits:

    src/octree/store.rs       (flint)
    src/render/renderer.rs    (onyx)
    src/render/svdag.rs       (onyx)
    src/sim/rule.rs           (flint)
    src/sim/world.rs          (flint)

Main.rs and rng.rs drift (ember's lane) was cleaned up in-session on 2026-04-11 by ember (hash-thing-?-fmt-cleanup commit).

## Why this is a P2 and not a P3 nit

Fmt drift is load-bearing for *other* policies. Any crew running `cargo fmt` as part of normal work produces a massive incidental diff that mixes with their intended change, poisoning `git blame` and making reviews noisy. The 88d scout review already flagged this: flint's branch contains +126 lines in renderer.rs and +132 in svdag.rs that are **pure cargo fmt reformatting, not logic** — the exact failure mode an fmt gate would have prevented.

## The plan (two-phase landing)

### Phase A: warn-only step (this bead)

Add a step to ci.yml's build job (or a dedicated fmt job parallel to actionlint):

```yaml
- name: Cargo fmt check (warn-only until drift clears)
  run: cargo fmt --check
  continue-on-error: true
```

Same shape as the existing clippy step (warn-only until 00f). Surfaces drift in CI logs on every push so crews can see which files still drift, without breaking builds. Put it on the ubuntu-24.04 leg only — rustfmt output is deterministic across platforms, no need to run it on mac and windows.

### Phase B: flip to hard-fail (follow-up bead)

Once store.rs / renderer.rs / svdag.rs / rule.rs / world.rs all fmt cleanly (either via lane-specific cleanup commits or via flint/onyx absorbing the fix into their next regular PR on those files), remove continue-on-error and promote the step to gating. Can happen via comment / scout review on the next flint or onyx commit that touches one of those files, or as an explicit sweep bead.

## Non-goals for this bead

- **Do NOT do a cargo fmt --all sweep as part of this.** That's a cross-lane surgical change and the 88d scout review explicitly called fmt-reformatting on out-of-lane files a predictable source of merge friction. The warn-only step lets drift clear organically as each file's owning crew touches it.
- **Do NOT couple to hash-thing-00f (clippy cleanup).** Separate concern, separate gate, separate landing. Clippy warn-only flips to hard-fail when the clippy backlog clears; fmt is a different backlog.

## Files

- .github/workflows/ci.yml — add the step
- No source code changes (that's the point — this is pure CI infra)

## Validation

- `cargo fmt --check` locally shows the 5 files drifting (repro)
- After ci.yml edit, actionlint clean
- After merge, next CI run surfaces the drift in the build log without red X

## Lane

Ember (generalist CI infra — xb7 epic sibling, extends xb7.1). Not claiming from this bead since I'm also filing a companion commit that adds the warn-only step in-session; update status if I get to it.

Filed from the autopilot scout ladder step 2.
