title: Attention-registering response header ritual
description:
## Goal

Every user message, Claude opens its response with a one-line **attention header** that registers attention on CLAUDE.md directives. The header lists all always-on directives as short phrases (not tags), plus any situational directives active this turn. The point is genuine attention placement — a ritual small enough not to bleed token budget but explicit enough to keep the directives live.

## User's verbatim framing

"We're going to try a new thing with CloudMD instructions or memory where every time there's a user message you always respond in a format that sort of registers that you're listening, that your attention is on certain directives, that it's maintained. And we want to do some balance between having you do a ritual to actually place that attention and minimizing tokens or attention required to follow the formatting overall. I'm willing to consider other memory formats in the future, but make a bead for all this and then do it. Or do some of it anyway."

Follow-up refinements:
- "We want instructions for this to live in global CloudMD."
- Drop the "listening" word — redundant.
- Drop the "turn framing" phrase — unclear and redundant with the directive list.
- Only one checkpoint, not both start and end. Start only, for now.
- "Ideally we surface **all** directives. Two to three relevant ones sounds super weak. Or at least, a little too weak."
- "Take out the no listening preamble / turn summary bits because nobody would do that to start."
- "What do you mean by tags?" — tags are single-word shorthand labels (e.g. `push-up`, `bias-to-action`) that refer to a directive without re-describing it. They compress attention away to rote; short phrases re-engage the behavior.

## Current format (landed in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md)

`⟨ offer ownership up · bias to action · note patterns to CLAUDE.md · parallelize when independent · diagram proactively ⟩`

- 5 always-on directives as short phrases (not tags). Gloss for each lives in CLAUDE.md.
- Add situational directives (dialogue, loopy, review, diagram triggered, etc.) at the end per turn.
- Push-up offer comes on the next line.
- **Explicitly excluded** from the header — stay implicit background hygiene: output efficiency, brilliant-impatient voice, no emojis, plain-text file refs, narrow-first lookups, recognize thrashing, bd workflow. Trimmed on user direction; full 11-item list was too heavy.

## Design principles captured

- **Phrases not tags** — tags decay to rote; phrases re-engage behavior. The ritual is genuine only if the form resists compression.
- **All, not relevant subset** — "relevant 2-3" is too weak because it invites the model to skip directives by declaring them irrelevant. Full list forces full pass.
- **Start-only** — one checkpoint for now. Might add end-of-turn later if attention-placement needs closing the loop.
- **Lean CLAUDE.md** — the global file explicitly says every token loads every session, so the directive about this ritual must itself be compact.

## Open questions / things to watch

- Does the full-list header actually stay fresh, or does it become a ~45-token dead band the model skims? If the latter, iterate format.
- Should situational directives be annotated as such, or mixed in?
- Should the ritual interact with `/diagram`, `/ship`, review-tiers skill loading, etc.?
- User is willing to consider **other memory formats** in the future — this is an experiment, not a final answer.

## Possible extensions (not doing yet)

- **Rotation / anti-decay tricks.** A fixed 11-item list risks the mind learning the *shape* and skipping content. Mitigations to try if decay shows up: shuffle order each turn, bold one directive at random, slightly rephrase one per turn, or cycle which directive gets emphasis. Forces re-parsing. Cheap to add. Deliberately deferred — try the static list first, only iterate if decay is observed.

## Related

- hash-thing-yls — broader exploration of better ways to interact with Claude Code.
close_reason: Format landed in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (5 always-on directives, exclusion list documented). Live for several turns. Possible rotation extension noted but deferred per user. Natural stopping point.

title: Explore better ways to interact with Claude Code
description:
User's verbatim framing (preserved):

"I want you to note this down somewhere as a bead, ideally. I don't know quite how... I don't know like a good way to interact with Claude, with Claude code. We have some basic things. But I think we can probably find something better. And in kind of a lot of ways, I basically think there's like a lot of room for improvement. There's a way to use Claude where I'm just spending a lot of attention hand-holding, walking through tasks. That's pretty weak. there's a way to use Claude where I'm I have like 10 or 20 agents doing a million things but they're all gated on like hard-coded attention steps for me that's great I would love if it were that simple I think in practice I'd aim for that and it doesn't get very near to that and then there's a way to use Claude where things are either a lot more things are fully autonomous or attention is a carefully, like human attention is a carefully spent resource and I think the latter is the ideal but I say ideal and I think that there might be just other ways of doing all this coding projects and I would like to at least explore this a bit but also aim higher and keep this in mind possibly within sessions sporadically I don't know so yeah, make a beat for all this try to preserve my copy And let's make sure to work on this at some point."

## Three modes identified

1. **Hand-holding mode** — spending lots of attention walking Claude through tasks step by step. Weak.
2. **Many-agents-gated mode** — 10-20 agents working in parallel, gated on hard-coded attention steps from the user. Would be great if it were that simple; aimed for in practice but doesn't get very near.
3. **Autonomous / attention-as-scarce-resource mode** — either mostly fully autonomous, or human attention is treated as a carefully spent resource. Stated ideal — but "ideal" is hedged: there may be other ways of approaching coding projects entirely.

## What to do

- Explore this space, aim higher than current defaults
- Keep this in mind sporadically within sessions (revisit the meta-question as work happens)
- Actually work on this at some point — not just a note
- Consider: what tools, workflows, harness tweaks, or entirely different approaches would move us toward the ideal mode

## Concrete near-term direction (added after follow-up)

User rejected a per-session running-log approach. Preferred direction: **with a few more hours of work on this project, do a review of Claude Code transcripts.** Read the actual session logs, look for where attention was spent well vs wastefully, where mode-2 broke down, what mode-3 would have looked like in hindsight. Let the data drive the meta-analysis rather than speculating in advance.

Transcripts for this project live under: `~/.claude/projects/-Users-edward-projects-hash-thing*/` (one dir per worktree, `.jsonl` files per session).

## Open questions

- What makes mode 2 (many-agents) fail in practice? Coordination overhead? Bad task decomposition? Attention bottleneck at merge?
- What does "attention as carefully spent resource" look like operationally?
- Are there non-Claude-shaped approaches worth exploring?

title: Flat renderer normals sampled from raw packed cell, not material
description:
**Discovered by:** Codex (broad sweep) in the Trident review of d7c5b4d (hash-thing-1v0.1, 16-bit cells).

## Problem

`src/render/raycast.wgsl:102-117` computes surface normals via a central-difference gradient on the **raw packed cell word** — not the decoded material id:

```wgsl
let val = textureLoad(volume, voxel_coord, 0).r; // u16 packed
// ...
let nx = f32(textureLoad(volume, voxel_coord + eps, 0).r) -
         f32(textureLoad(volume, voxel_coord - eps, 0).r);
```

Before the 16-bit move this was fine — `val` was a u8 material id, identical to the palette index. After 1v0.1, `val` is `(material << 6) | metadata`, so the "gradient" now reflects metadata transitions too. Two voxels of the same material with different metadata will produce a bogus normal and a visible fake lighting edge.

## Fix

Decode material before taking the gradient. Either:
1. Read `val >> 6u` instead of `val` for each of the 6 samples, OR
2. Introduce a helper `fn decode_material(raw: u32) -> u32 { raw >> METADATA_BITS; }` and use it everywhere palette-adjacent code touches raw cell words.

Option 2 is better long-term (single source of truth in the shader, mirrors the Rust `Cell::material()` method, easier to keep in sync with the METADATA_BITS drift guard added in 1v0.1 fix).

## Scope

- `src/render/raycast.wgsl:102-117` (gradient sampling)
- Any other place in either shader that consumes raw cell words for non-palette purposes. Audit during the fix.

## Notes

The flat renderer is only used in debug/fallback mode — the SVDAG shader is the primary path — but shipping garbage normals is still user-visible once metadata gets used by even one material (planned for 1v0.2). Filed as P2: ugly but not corruption.

Filed as a discovered-from dependency of hash-thing-1v0.1.

title: Implement lazy root expansion behind World::ensure_contains
description:
Implement the body of World::ensure_contains(x, y, z), which today is a stub that unimplemented!()s with a pointer to SPEC.md.

Goal: when called with a coordinate outside the current root, grow the root by wrapping it in successively bigger parent nodes until the coordinate is in-bounds, generating empty siblings on the way up.

Algorithm sketch (see SPEC.md "Lazy root expansion" section landed by hash-thing-819):
1. While x >= side || y >= side || z >= side (or any negative if signed):
   a. Compute the octant the existing root sits in within the new bigger parent. Convention: existing root is the (-x, -y, -z) corner child (octant 0) of the new parent at level+1.
   b. New parent's other 7 children are empty(level) nodes (already canonical via hash-cons).
   c. self.root = self.store.interior(level + 1, [old_root, empty, empty, empty, empty, empty, empty, empty]).
   d. self.level += 1.
2. Once contained, return.

Open questions to resolve in this ticket:
- step_cache invalidation: do existing cached step results survive a root expansion? (Yes, because cached entries are keyed by NodeId, and the old root's NodeId still exists and still steps to the same result. The new parent has its own cache entries.)
- raycaster bounds: the renderer uses self.level to compute world extent. Renderer must observe the new level after expansion. Today renderers re-upload each frame so this is automatic, but the SVDAG renderer has a build step that must rerun.
- far-field probe protection: only writes go through ensure_contains. Reads stay pure (return 0 outside the realized root). Verify spark's terrain layer agrees.
- coordinate sign: the current API is u64. If we want to grow in -x, -y, -z directions we either need i64 coords or a recentering scheme. Defer or address here.
- World::side() semantics after expansion: it returns the realized region size, not a hard limit. Update doc comment.

Tests:
- ensure_contains grows root to exactly contain a far coordinate (no over-shoot).
- Existing cells survive expansion (their world-coordinates are stable iff we use the corner-child convention).
- Multiple expansions compound correctly.
- ensure_contains is a no-op when the coord is already in-bounds.

Blocked-by: hash-thing-819 (which lands the stub and SPEC.md design section).

title: Crew coordination: /ship-by-default + plan-review gating
description:
Track the cross-cutting effort to make `/ship` the hard default for hash-thing crew (flint, spark, ember, onyx, cairn), and to design when plan-review should gate work.

## Problem
Crew members are not respecting the "every roadmap task via /ship" rule in CLAUDE.md:
- Some seem to be dropping straight to implement without the dialogue/plan/review gates
- /ship is not stopping for human approval before pushing/closing on things that needed a plan
- Unclear when plan-review should fire (not every task needs 9-way trident, but some do)

## Desired behavior
- `/ship` (not `/ship-auto`) is the default entry point for every roadmap task
- Plan-review tier is selected per task, not hardcoded:
  - P1 invariant-bearing / foundational work → plan + code both reviewed (bigger tier)
  - P2 straightforward features → plan-review single, code-review single or dual
  - P3 nits/cleanup → no plan-review, code-review single
- Plan approval is a real human gate. Crew must stop before implementing when the plan is non-trivial
- Keep the bias toward bigger code reviews (~half Trident) from global CLAUDE.md

## Open questions
- Who decides the tier — the agent proposing it, or a lookup by beads priority/label?
- Should plan-review be skipped only on `--from=implement` explicit flag?
- How do we audit compliance? (bd comments? commit trailer? ship metadata file?)

## Scope
Design + document + enforce. Likely outputs:
- Updated project CLAUDE.md with crew list, /ship default policy, tier selection rules
- Possibly a ship wrapper hook that logs tier decision to the beads task
- Onboarding message template crew gets on session start

title: Add unit test suite for NodeStore hash-cons / set_cell / from_flat roundtrip
description:
Code review important I6 (Claude Standard).

src/octree/store.rs — zero unit tests. intern_node, set_cell, get_cell, flatten, from_flat, step_cache are all untested. Only src/render/svdag.rs has tests and those exercise the store indirectly.

Hash-consing is load-bearing for Hashlife recursive stepping (SPEC.md:205 — the entire memory story depends on it). Without tests, a regression in intern_node or from_flat_recursive goes unnoticed until a demo looks wrong. The upcoming Stepper / Hashlife work will lean hard on interior() canonicalization and there is currently nothing that would catch 'two structurally-equal subtrees produce two different NodeIds'.

TEST SUITE (minimum):
1. intern-dedup: building the same Node twice returns the same id
2. from_flat then flatten roundtrips
3. set_cell + get_cell roundtrip at multiple levels
4. empty subtree shares id regardless of build path (uniform(level, 0) == interior of empty children)
5. population sum property: sum of populations of 8 children == population of parent
6. structural equality: interior built from the same 8 children returns the same NodeId regardless of order of insertion

These also double as property-test scaffolding for the Stepper trait refactor — the intern-dedup + structural-equality tests are what will catch Hashlife memoization bugs.
close_reason: 15-test suite landed as 56034c1. Covers intern dedup (leaf + interior), empty-subtree dedup across 3 build paths, population fold-up, set/get roundtrip at level 3 and level 6, from_flat/flatten roundtrip, from_flat dedup and population match, structural equality (same grid → same root id), empty_world_is_compact canary, step_cache insert/lookup/clear, stats tracking. 32/32 tests pass workspace-wide. Unblocks hash-thing-6gf.1 recursive step — the intern_dedup_interior + structurally_equal_trees_share_root tests are what will catch Hashlife memoization bugs if a canonicalization regression slips in.

title: Svdag::build panics on leaf root + from_flat trusts non-power-of-2 side
description:
Code review important I4 + I5 (combined — both are API hygiene on the Svdag build path).

I4 — src/render/svdag.rs:43-56,79 — Svdag::build accepts any root NodeId but write_node panics on Node::Leaf. Comment hand-waves as 'root will always be level >= 1 in practice'. Future optimizations (root collapse, single-cell test fixtures, regression scenes) can legitimately produce a leaf root.

FIX: serialize a degenerate one-node representation for leaf roots (emit a single interior with all 8 children pointing at the leaf), or reject at the public API boundary with a typed error. Add a regression test.

I5 — src/render/svdag.rs:184-187 and src/octree/store.rs:184 — (side as f64).log2() as u32 silently truncates on non-power-of-2. For side=48, log2=5.58, as u32=5 → builds a level-5 (32^3) tree over 48^3 data, reads past the buffer.

FIX: assert!(side.is_power_of_two()) + side.trailing_zeros() instead of log2.

Both will bite chunked-world work in different ways. Fix together in the same ship.

title: Rule preset constructors disagree with comments and UI labels
description:
Code review important I3 (Codex Critical, novel finding).

src/sim/rule.rs:10-49 — the comments describe one set of rules (4/4 crystals, 2..6/5..7 amoeba) but the constructors implement different ranges:
  - amoeba() = 9..26/5..7
  - crystal() = 0..6/1..3
  - rule445() = actual 4/4
Meanwhile src/main.rs:129-143 logs these constructors as 'Amoeba', 'Crystal', '445' without any numeric disambiguation.

Impact: if someone toggles between presets while debugging memoization, render bugs, or pattern behavior, they are comparing mislabeled automata. Contaminates screenshots, debugging sessions, and any future rule-id keying for step_cache (will make 6gf.0 silently wrong).

FIX:
- Pick one source of truth for preset numeric ranges.
- Align the constructor code + comments + main.rs log labels.
- Add unit tests that assert each preset's numeric ranges and display label.

This should land together with the step_cache rule-keying fix (hash-thing-6gf.0) so cache keys don't key on ambiguous labels.

title: seed_center underflows u64 for radius > center
description:
Code review important I2 (unanimous: Claude Standard + Critical + Codex Standard).

src/sim/world.rs:68-83 — seed_center loops over (center - radius)..(center + radius) using u64. With center = side/2 = 8 (level-4 world) and radius=12 (demo default), center - radius underflows. Debug panics; release wraps to 0xFF..FFFC..20 producing an 18-quintillion-iteration loop.

Trigger: World::new(4).seed_center(12, 0.35) — panics or hangs immediately.

FIX: clamp loop bounds to [0, side):
  let lo = center.saturating_sub(radius);
  let hi = (center + radius).min(side);

Add a unit test: World::new(3).seed_center(8, 0.5) should succeed with clipped seed region.

title: Renderer render() return value ignored + hot-spin on Occluded window
description:
Code review important I1 (Claude Standard + Critical).

src/main.rs:213 and src/render/renderer.rs:385-393 — render() returns bool (false on Outdated/Lost/Timeout/Occluded) but the main loop discards it. On Occluded the renderer returns without submitting, yet the caller still requests the next redraw. Combined with ControlFlow::Poll, minimize/screen-lock on macOS spins CPU to 100%.

Also the '_ => return false' catch-all swallows any future wgpu SurfaceTexture variant silently, which will hide real errors during GPU debugging.

FIX:
- Drop the catch-all so new variants become compile errors.
- On Occluded, stop requesting redraws until WindowEvent::Occluded(false) fires.
- Log at error level on Lost/Timeout.
- Either surface the bool return (main checks it) or drop the return type entirely.

title: Determinism audit (no global PRNG, no scan order)
description: Walk the codebase, flag any global PRNG, thread_rng, or order-dependent iteration inside simulation or terrain-gen. Determinism is sacred.
close_reason: Audit complete; no corruption found. Two minor follow-ups filed: h34-99e (step_cache doc/key mismatch, dormant under brute-force path, real under 6gf.1) and h34-c6k (seed_center → cell_rand_bool migration now that h34.1 landed). Audit notes in .ship-notes/ship-h34.2-determinism-audit.md. Sim path is determinism-clean as of 94c66c0.

title: Perf measurement infrastructure
description: Per-operation timing + memory counters for step, terrain gen, DAG serialization. Surface on-screen overlay or log at intervals.

title: hash(position, generation, seed) PRNG utility
description: Deterministic per-cell randomness. Required for probabilistic CA to remain Hashlife-compatible.
close_reason: cell_hash PRNG utility landed in 8b7e469 + c970287 (review fixes). 13 unit tests covering determinism, avalanche across all 5 input channels, f64/bool uniformity, Lemire range bounds, and Hashlife memoization sanity. Module ready to consume from Margolus movement phase (1v0.4), probabilistic CA rules, and terrain-gen.

title: CI matrix: Mac + Linux + Windows builds
description: GitHub Actions matrix with cargo build --release for all three target triples. Cache target/ and cargo registry.

title: HashDAG-style incremental edit uploads
description: Upload only the nodes changed per simulation step, not the whole DAG. Embeds the DAG in a GPU hash table per Careil/Billeter/Eisemann 2020.

title: Runtime render-path toggle (flat <-> DAG)
description: Keyboard toggle between the existing flat 3D-texture raycaster and the new DAG raycaster. Keeps both paths working during development.
close_reason: V-key toggle implementation landed; SVDAG shader correctness tracked separately under 5bb.2 + 5eq bug

title: Per-leaf material attributes in DAG
description: Extend DAG leaves to carry material IDs. Shader looks up material properties via the GPU material registry (from E2).

title: Lazy root expansion for infinite worlds
description: When the player reaches the edge of the current root, wrap the root in a bigger parent whose other 7 children are default-terrain nodes, generated on demand.

title: Terrain-gen perf tracking
description: Per-region timing and memory accounting during generation. Flag if noise eval becomes the bottleneck; consider SIMD noise or CA-based gen as escape hatches.

title: Multi-resolution procedural gen(node_region) -> NodeId
description: Evaluate noise at region corners; if uniform, return uniform node without descending. Only subdivide where noise transitions occur (surface, caves, biome boundaries). Octree-native, not chunk-flat.

title: Gravity and fluid flow via Margolus rules
description: Density-based swap rules: heavier-over-lighter swaps toward the heavier direction. Water spreads laterally with a bias. Sand falls. Lava flows slowly.

title: Hashlife vs brute-force correctness harness
description: Property test: random seed -> N steps via brute-force grid == N steps via recursive Hashlife. Run across all four GoL presets and (later) material CA rules.

title: Margolus parity threaded through recursion
description: Phase 2 (movement) uses Margolus 2x2x2 blocks whose partition offset alternates by generation parity. Thread parity through the recursive step so memoization key is (NodeId, phase, parity).

title: Foundations & determinism
description: Cross-cutting: determinism audit (hash(pos,gen,seed) randomness, no global PRNG), perf measurement infra, replace Game of Life scaffolding.

title: Cross-platform distribution
description: Native binaries for Mac/Linux/Windows via CI matrix, macOS notarization, AppImage, optional WASM web build, Steam deferred.

title: Terrain generation & infinite worlds
description: Multi-resolution noise-driven generation operating at octree-node granularity, cave smoothing, dungeons, infinite worlds via lazy root expansion.

title: Wall-clock auto-log cadence for Perf (replace generation % 10 coupling)
description: Currently main.rs logs perf summary on 'generation % 10 == 0' which ties the log rate to the step rate — when the sim is paused or stepping slowly, the log goes quiet. Absorb sleepy's 2-second auto-log pattern: poll a wall-clock timer in the redraw loop and emit a summary every N seconds independent of generation. Preserve the existing per-10-gen logline for step-aligned output if useful, or replace it. Small, self-contained.

title: Add memory-watchdog metric family (node count + step_cache peaks) to Perf
description: Port the approach from sleepy-wiggling-fountain's 462d764 src/perf.rs: track NodeStore node count and step_cache size with ratcheting peaks, plus memory_bytes_estimate() using size_of::<Node>() and size_of::<NodeId>(). This is orthogonal to the latency tracking vast's Perf already does — add as a second metric family (PerfMem or similar) so the OOM alarm signal isn't lost. Tests: peak never retreats, memory estimate scales linearly with node count.

title: Clippy/-D warnings drift on main (13 warnings, 6 files)
description:
# Clippy/warning drift on main

## Summary

`cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` currently fails on main with 13 distinct warnings across 6 files. `cargo build` and `cargo test --no-run` still succeed (these are warnings, not errors) but any /ship that runs clippy-D-warnings as a gate is blocked.

## Scope breakdown by file

**Unlocked (cairn-shippable):**
- `src/main.rs` x2 — `manual_is_multiple_of` at lines 60 and 201 (both `generation % 10 == 0` logging gates)
- `src/octree/mod.rs` x1 — unused imports `octant_coords`, `octant_index`
- `src/sim/mod.rs` x1 — unused import `CaRule`

**Locked by in-flight branches:**
- `src/octree/node.rs` x1 — dead-code methods `level`, `is_empty`, `side_len` (flint / 1v0.1 lane)
- `src/octree/store.rs` x5 — dead-code methods + naming lint `from_*` takes no self (cairn / 819 lane, parked)
- `src/sim/world.rs` x2 — dead-code `get` + `collapsible_if` at line 75 (cairn / 819 lane, parked)
- `src/render/svdag.rs` x7 — loop-variable-used-to-index, dead-code fields, misc (onyx / render lane)

## Root cause (hypothesis)

- **Rust 1.94 added new clippy lints** (`manual_is_multiple_of`, `collapsible_if` tightened, etc.) — latent issues became warnings on toolchain upgrade.
- **Dead-code warnings** come from unwired scaffolding: methods/fields/imports added in anticipation of future callers that haven't landed yet (Hashlife stepper, material tags, chunk streaming). These are all expected to be consumed by in-flight branches but currently warn on main.

## Why this is tech debt, not a blocker

`cargo build` and `cargo test` both pass. The binary runs. Only `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` fails. So downstream development works; it's the quality-gate side of /ship that trips.

## Fix options

- **A. Narrow fix + stopgap allow.** Fix the 4 unlocked-file warnings (main.rs x2, mod.rs imports x2), add crate-level `#![allow(...)]` in main.rs for the lint categories that still fire on locked files. Ships a green clippy baseline without touching locked files. Stopgap must be removed as each owner lands their cleanup.
- **B. Broad fix.** One cairn branch fixes all 13 warnings. Low per-site conflict risk (dead-code removals + mechanical rewrites) but coordination cost with flint/onyx/cairn-819. File-lock convention says don't.
- **C. Per-owner bead splits.** This bead becomes the epic; file cleanup subtasks for each owner to absorb into their in-flight branch (flint fixes node.rs dead code as part of 1v0.1; onyx fixes svdag.rs as part of their next render bead; cairn fixes store.rs/world.rs as part of 819 when unparked). Slow but clean.
- **D. Do nothing.** Accept `-D warnings` clippy is red until branches land; reroute /ship validate to plain `cargo clippy` without `-D warnings` as a project-CLAUDE.md policy change.

## Recommendation

A + C combined: cairn ships the narrow fix + stopgap allow NOW (small, clean, unblocks /ship for everyone). This bead stays open as the tracking epic, with C as the long-term burndown plan. Owners remove their allow lines as they fix their files.

## Side finding

The 4 unlocked-file fixes are candidates for cairn-shipped branch `cairn/clippy-hygiene-unlocked` (fit for autonomous afk shipping).

## See also

- ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md ship skill validate step — runs `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` for Rust repos
- hash-thing-7ii (file-lock convention) — why this bead respects lock boundaries

title: Self-compact kickoff 'go' unreliably lands in post-compact input buffer
description:
# Self-compact kickoff flake

## Pattern

The CLAUDE.md self-compact mechanism (see "When blocked: poll, don't stop") requires a backgrounded kickoff that arrives after /compact completes:

```bash
SID="${CMUX_SURFACE_ID:-$CMUX_PANEL_ID}"
nohup bash -c "sleep N && cmux send --surface '$SID' \$'go\n'" >/dev/null 2>&1 & disown
cmux send --surface "$SID" $'/compact\n'
```

The kickoff `go\n` is supposed to land in the session's fresh post-compact input buffer and auto-resume the agent. It fails intermittently. When it fails, the session sits idle until a human (or cron) pokes it.

## Observed failures

1. Poll 8 (2026-04-11, 5s sleep) — kickoff didn't land; session idle until user poked it ("Hey, looks like your compact failed...").
2. Poll 9 (2026-04-11, 20s sleep) — kickoff didn't land again; same symptom. User: "looks like your compact failed... let's track it over time. reduce latency to 5 sec at most."

## Why sleep isn't the root cause

Bumping sleep 5s -> 20s did NOT fix it. Both durations fail. The hypothesis "compact rendering takes 10-30s and mid-render sends get dropped" was wrong.

## Hypotheses (unverified)

1. Buffer-not-attached: post-compact session's input buffer isn't wired to accept external cmux send until the first human keystroke / focus event. Kickoff arrives but the buffer that's listening isn't the one cmux send is writing to.
2. Dispatcher consumes it: the /compact slash dispatcher stays attached briefly post-compact and swallows subsequent input that looks like a continuation rather than a new turn.
3. Nohup subshell race: the backgrounded subshell is killed by SIGHUP despite nohup (on some cmux builds) or the pgroup is cleaned up when the original claude process exits.
4. cmux send flakes on self-directed sends: sending to the same surface that's running the sender has a race that cross-surface sends don't hit.

## Debug recipe

Next occurrence, capture:
- ps output of the nohup subshell pre- and post-compact to verify it survived
- od -c of the exact payload produced by $'go\n' expansion (verified once: produces g o \n \n correctly — escaping is not the issue)
- cmux logs around the send time to see if the send was acknowledged
- Time delta between /compact fire and session idle state
- Whether kickoff lands if sleep is very long (60s+) — would rule out race conditions vs. permanent buffer disconnect

## Mitigation (currently in CLAUDE.md)

"If cron fire is imminent (<5min), skip self-compact entirely — the cron's own prompt will land on whatever context you have."

This works but leaves accumulated context on small-sleep polls. Not a real fix.

## Fix options

- A. Different mechanism: trigger self-compact via a cron fire instead of in-turn. Self-fire a CronCreate one-shot that runs "/compact go" in 2 minutes; exit current turn cleanly. No nohup race.
- B. Post-compact hook: if Claude Code supports a post-compact hook (needs verification), use that to inject the kickoff atomically.
- C. Accept the flake: skip self-compact always; rely on cron delivering the next poll prompt. Costs some budget across polls but eliminates the failure mode.
- D. Long poll: sleep 60s+ and accept the dead time. Only viable if the root cause IS timing (which 20s disproves).

## Scope

Fix the mechanism so self-compact reliably resumes the session. Agent-infra / afk-loop reliability bug.

## See also

- ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md "When blocked: poll, don't stop" section — current pattern + "Self-compact kickoff is flaky" note added 2026-04-11
- hash-thing-0uw (background task hangs) — sibling agent-infra reliability bug

title: Fix .beads permissions warning (chmod 700) across worktrees
description:
Every `bd` invocation in this worktree (and likely others) prints:

```
Warning: /Users/edward/projects/hash-thing/.claude/worktrees/wiggly-nibbling-trinket/.beads has permissions 0755 (recommended: 0700). Run: chmod 700 /Users/edward/projects/hash-thing/.claude/worktrees/wiggly-nibbling-trinket/.beads
```

Noisy. Appears on every `bd show`, `bd create`, `bd list`, `bd update` call. Pollutes command output and agent stdout logs.

## Fix

Option A — run `chmod 700 .beads` in every worktree. One-liner per worktree. Permanent if the directory permissions stick through git operations.

```bash
find .claude/worktrees/*/.beads -type d -exec chmod 700 {} \;
chmod 700 .beads  # for the main repo root if applicable
```

Option B — add a post-checkout hook or worktree-creation helper that sets perms correctly from birth.

Option C — ignore (live with the warning).

Recommend A + B together: A fixes the current state, B prevents recurrence on new worktrees.

## Scope

- Apply chmod 700 to all current `.beads` directories across hash-thing worktrees.
- Verify `bd ready` output is clean (no warning).
- Optionally: add the perm-set to any worktree-spawning helper documented in project `CLAUDE.md` or the "Export AI coding setup" doc (`hash-thing-1ze`).

Not a production blocker, but every multi-agent session is leaking this to stdout and it's crowding real signal. Low priority, high satisfaction on close.

title: Rollback multiplayer for small-player competitive modes
description:
**Future optimization — rollback networking for small-player-count competitive modes.**

**The architectural gift:** hash-thing's hash-consed octree makes world snapshots O(1) (pointer-copy, not memcpy). Storing 60 seconds of world history at 60 Hz is 60*60*4 = 14,400 bytes. That's not a typo. Rollback's expensive step in every other engine (ECS-world snapshotting) is free here.

**Additionally:** rerunning `step_ca()` N frames is cheap under Hashlife memoization — shared subtrees hit the memo cache. Rollback's other expensive step (re-simulation) is also unusually cheap.

**Scope sketch:**
- Rollback loop: predict remote inputs, simulate forward, rewind + re-simulate on mismatch
- Input prediction per peer with speculative ordering
- Ring-buffer of `root: NodeId` snapshots (O(1) per frame)
- Re-simulation with memoization assistance
- Snap to authoritative state on divergence

**Player count ceiling:** 2-4 players. Rollback degrades badly with N>4 due to input-prediction cost.

**Who this serves:** 2-player PVP minigames, duel modes, combat-focused sandbox play. Not casual 16-player cooperative sessions (use lockstep for those — see hash-thing-3bj).

**Precedent:** none in voxel/CA space. hash-thing would be plausibly the first-ever rollback voxel game because the substrate uniquely makes snapshotting free.

**Do NOT claim until:**
- hash-thing-3bj (lockstep v1) ships and is proven
- At least one combat or PVP mode exists as a real gameplay target
- Edward has expressed interest in competitive modes

**See:** `.ship-notes/multiplayer-model-options.md` for the full architecture case.

title: Plugin surface Tier-2: Wasm component sandbox (future)
description:
**Future epic placeholder.** Sandboxed, capability-based plugin tier using the Wasm Component Model.

**Context:** survey `.ship-notes/plugin-surface-survey.md` recommends Option A (pure Rust crates / Tier 1) for v1, with Option D (hybrid) as the long-term target. This bead tracks the Tier-2 Wasm component work for when it becomes time.

**Scope sketch (not a spec):**
- WIT interfaces for: mutation channel, entity kinds, gameplay modes, recipes
- Host bindings via `wasmtime::component::bindgen!`
- Guest toolchain support for Rust (primary) and optionally other languages
- Capability-based security model: plugins import exactly the world-interaction operations they need; cannot escape
- Hot-reload / component swap story
- Deployment model: per-server plugin bundles shipped to clients (Luanti-style)

**Why this is P3:**
1. Tier 1 (Rust crates) must be proven out first — the trait shapes validated against real plugins before lifting them to WIT
2. No voxel game has shipped with this model; we'd be the first, so expect tooling pain
3. `hash-thing-1v0.9` (mutation channel) must be designed WIT-compatible so this bead becomes "port, not redesign"

**Trigger to promote:**
- Untrusted third-party mods become a real requirement, OR
- A creator needs hot-reload iteration that Rust recompilation can't give them, OR
- Multiplayer (hash-thing-0et) lands with lockstep semantics and we want determinism-by-construction

**Do NOT claim this bead until 1v0.x is substantially done and at least two third-party contributors have tried writing Tier-1 plugins.**

See `.ship-notes/plugin-surface-survey.md` for full context.

title: SVDAG integer DDA traversal (Laine-Karras Stage 3)
description: Post-27m long-term item. The v1+v2 27m fix acknowledges integer DDA as the proper answer to the f32 frontier: ultra-grazing rays with |rd_axis| < ~1e-5 at MAX_DEPTH still collapse below ULP because dt_cap binds and the exit-axis advance goes sub-ULP. Both the commit message and inline comments explicitly defer to integer DDA. Scope: replace world-space pos = ro + rd*t recomputation with per-axis tmax/tdelta incremental state + canonical cell coordinates at max depth. Amanatides-Woo style DDA generalized to hierarchical octants. Should be done AFTER hash-thing-5c4 (ray-octant mirroring) since mirroring simplifies the incremental math. After this lands, all ULP concerns vanish because dt is computed in cell units, not world units. Blocks hash-thing-4rj (ancestor memoization) — exact cache keys require integer traversal.

title: GPU timestamp queries for render + upload wall-clock timing
description:
src/perf.rs caveat 1 documents that current timings around wgpu queue submissions / Queue::write_* measure *CPU submit overhead*, not GPU execution time — the metrics are explicitly named *_cpu (render_cpu, upload_cpu) to surface this honestly.

Ticket opened per the module's own TODO: 'Real GPU timing belongs in a future bead with timestamp queries.' (perf.rs:13)

## Why it matters for this project

Hash-thing's whole scaling story is GPU-bound once we leave the 64³ demo. Flat 3D texture raycaster is already fragment-shader-bound on anything bigger than ~256³, and the SVDAG traversal shader (5bb) is going to be where most of the frame budget lives by the time 4096³ is interactive. Pretending the render_cpu number reflects GPU cost would be a lie of omission once we start optimizing the shaders.

## What this bead ships

1. Enable `wgpu::Features::TIMESTAMP_QUERY` when creating the device in src/render/renderer.rs. Graceful fallback: if the adapter doesn't support it (some older MoltenVK setups) fall back to the current CPU-only perf ring and log one-time "GPU timestamps unavailable on this adapter".

2. Allocate a small QuerySet (2 entries: render start + end, can grow to cover upload later) and a readback buffer, plumb begin/write_timestamp at the start and end of RenderPass scope, resolve to the readback buffer at frame end.

3. Extend src/perf.rs with a new metric family `*_gpu` (e.g. `render_gpu`, `upload_gpu`) that accepts already-resolved `Duration` values. Keep the `*_cpu` metrics unchanged so the distinction stays explicit in the per-gen summary line.

4. Thread the readback into App::window_event::RedrawRequested after render. Because wgpu readbacks are async (map_async), stage one frame behind: this frame's render records into the CPU perf ring at CPU-submit time AND enqueues the timestamp readback; next frame we poll map_async and record the previous frame's actual GPU duration. Drop samples that are still unmapped when the next frame lands.

5. Add a single sanity test: build a no-op pipeline, submit one render pass wrapped in timestamps, poll to completion, assert the resolved duration is > 0 and < something absurdly large. Not trying to verify absolute accuracy, just that the pipeline plumbing is alive.

## Caveats to document in the commit

- Timestamp resolution varies by GPU; some adapters report in 1ns units, others in 100ns units. wgpu exposes the period as `Queue::get_timestamp_period()`. Don't assume nanoseconds.
- metal backend (mac): TIMESTAMP_QUERY_INSIDE_PASSES requires MTLCounterSamplingPoint support, which is not available on all older Intel macs. Adapter check before enabling.
- wasm32 / WebGPU: TIMESTAMP_QUERY is not yet stable in the WebGPU spec as of 2026-04-11. Feature-gate the GPU timing path on `#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))]`. Web build falls back to CPU-only.

## Lane / priority

Ember (generalist perf infra, owns perf.rs) — landing the timestamp pipeline itself. Touching renderer.rs for the QuerySet plumbing is a soft lane crossing but perf instrumentation is explicitly called out as 'lives in main.rs / perf.rs, not in sim/render/octree code' in the perf.rs module doc, so the minimal hook into renderer.rs is a cross-lane invariant, not onyx-lane feature work.

P3 — nice-to-have, not blocking any active scaling milestone. Becomes P2 once 5bb.5 (HashDAG incremental edits) lands and we're actually profiling SVDAG shader variants against each other.

Filed by ember from autopilot scout ladder step 2.

title: Test coverage: src/octree/node.rs pure helpers
description:
Parallel to hash-thing-nna (world.rs) and hash-thing-1di (rule.rs): src/octree/node.rs has zero direct unit tests despite exposing four pure helpers that the whole tree builder assumes round-trip correctly.

## Functions needing coverage

### Pure free functions
- `octant_index(x, y, z) -> usize` — line 60
- `octant_coords(idx) -> (u32, u32, u32)` — line 66

### Node methods
- `Node::level(&self) -> u32` — leaf returns 0, interior returns stored level
- `Node::is_empty(&self) -> bool` — leaf sees state==0, interior sees population==0
- `Node::side_len(&self) -> u64` — 1 << level

## Suggested tests (all pure, no store setup needed)

1. `octant_index_matches_formula` — exhaustive over all 8 (x,y,z) ∈ {0,1}³, assert indices 0..8 in the exact documented order (the doc comment at node.rs:17-25 is a load-bearing contract — store.rs:126, store.rs:147 index into child arrays assuming that exact bit layout).

2. `octant_coords_is_inverse_of_octant_index` — for idx in 0..8, `octant_index(octant_coords(idx)) == idx`. This is the one-liner that catches any future `>> 1` vs `>> 2` typo in either direction.

3. `octant_roundtrip_coordinates` — for all (x,y,z) ∈ {0,1}³, `octant_coords(octant_index(x,y,z)) == (x,y,z)`. Both directions of the roundtrip because they're separately maintained.

4. `leaf_level_is_zero` — `Node::Leaf(5).level() == 0`, `Node::Leaf(0).level() == 0`.

5. `interior_level_echoes_field` — construct a Node::Interior with level=3 and assert .level() == 3.

6. `leaf_is_empty_iff_zero_state` — `Leaf(0).is_empty()` true, `Leaf(1).is_empty()` false, `Leaf(255).is_empty()` false. Catches the payload-aware emptiness semantics (a leaf with material=42 is NOT empty even though CellState::0 is the 'empty' sentinel).

7. `interior_is_empty_iff_population_zero` — population=0 → empty, population=1 → not empty. Note: is_empty is the store's quick emptiness check — if this flips sign, compaction and DAG build both break.

8. `side_len_is_power_of_two` — level 0 → 1, level 1 → 2, level 5 → 32, level 10 → 1024. Checks the bit-shift matches the documented 2^level.

## Why it matters

octant_index is called from store.rs:126 and store.rs:147 on every subtree build. octant_coords is called from store.rs:184 and store.rs:231 on every store traversal. A silent off-by-one in either one produces rotated / mirrored children everywhere and the tests we have (raycaster visuals, compaction invariants) would eventually catch it but slowly and expensively. Direct tests fail in < 1ms and pinpoint the break immediately.

## Lane / priority

P3 hygiene — pure helpers, non-blocking, good first-bead for flint's lane (same lane as hash-thing-nna and hash-thing-1di, the test-gap triad).

Filed by ember from the autopilot scout ladder, step 2 (file new beads). Not claiming — flint's lane.

title: Unit tests for sim::rule::GameOfLife3D step_cell (boundary + preset coverage)
description:
Scout finding (ember, autopilot dry-queue pass). Filed as a sibling to hash-thing-nna (sim/world.rs test coverage).

## Why

`src/sim/rule.rs` is 71 lines of pure CA rule logic — zero I/O, zero state, trivially unit-testable — and currently has zero tests. Filing so that when flint (or the next sim-lane worker) is in the module for 1v0.3 (hand-authored interaction table) or 6gf.1 (recursive step), the boundary behavior is pinned in place before any refactor.

## Scope (concrete test list)

1. **Dead cell with zero neighbors stays dead** across all 4 presets. All preset `birth_min >= 1`, so 0 neighbors can never birth. Sanity check.
2. **Crystal preset design quirk**: `survive_min = 0` means a live cell with zero neighbors survives. Pin this behavior explicitly so a future author doesn't 'fix' it.
3. **Amoeba preset**: `survive_min = 9` means isolated live cells die immediately.
4. **Survive range boundary — rule445** (survive 4..=4): n=3 dies, n=4 survives, n=5 dies. Catches off-by-one inside the range check.
5. **Birth range boundary — rule445** (birth 4..=4): same three cases on a dead center.
6. **Payload transparency on survival**: `step_cell(42, all-zero-neighbors)` with crystal preset returns 42 (not 1), because survival returns `center`, not a constant. Locks in the invariant 1v0.2 will rely on when CellState carries material tags.
7. **Nonzero-neighbor aliveness**: populate the neighbor array with distinct nonzero values (1, 7, 42, 255) and confirm `if n > 0` counts them all. Guards against a future refactor that compares against a specific material ID.
8. **All-26-neighbors-alive preset survival**: amoeba survives (9..=26 includes 26); crystal/rule445/pyroclastic die (26 is outside their survive ranges). One assertion per preset.

Target: ~9 tests, ~60 lines at the bottom of rule.rs. All pure function evaluations, zero setup, runs in microseconds.

## Lane

flint (sim). Filing not claiming — same rationale as nna. Pure test additions are borderline ember-safe but the consistent pattern is to file and let the lane owner pick up.

## Priority

P3. Same priority tier as nna (test infra for a module that's about to be touched by P1 work upstream).

title: Unit tests for brute-force sim::world step_flat (reference for 6gf.3 correctness harness)
description:
Scout finding (ember, autopilot dry-queue pass).

## Why this matters now

hash-thing-6gf.3 is planned as a property-test harness comparing the recursive Hashlife step against the current brute-force step_flat as a reference implementation. Philosophically: if the brute-force has latent bugs, 6gf.3 is building on sand — any recursive step that matches the brute-force will replicate the bug. The harness catches regressions in 6gf.1 relative to the reference, but it can't catch a bug that exists in the reference itself.

Currently `src/sim/world.rs` has zero unit tests. The module lacks pinned golden outputs that would let a future 6gf.1 author (flint) know the brute-force is working correctly at the moment of the refactor.

## Scope

Add a #[cfg(test)] module inside src/sim/world.rs covering at minimum:

1. **Empty world stays empty.** step_flat on an all-empty world produces an all-empty world (no false-positive births from the neighbor scan).
2. **Single live cell dies (GoL 'amoeba' rule).** One cell at the origin has zero live neighbors, so it dies next tick. Confirms no off-by-one in the 3x3x3 neighbor window.
3. **Known stable 3D pattern.** Pick one small deterministic still-life for the amoeba or crystal preset (hand-compute one via the 3D rule table) and assert it survives N steps without drift.
4. **Known oscillator.** Same as above but a period-2 pattern that alternates correctly.
5. **Determinism across repeated runs.** Same seed + same rule + same starting grid → same ending grid, byte-for-byte via store.flatten equality.
6. **Boundary handling.** Live cells at the grid edge do NOT consider cells outside the world (no wraparound or off-by-one reads past the side). Assert via a cell placed at (side-1, side-1, side-1) with all neighbors dead → still dies.

Tests should use a small grid (side=8 or side=16) so they run fast. Use level=3 or level=4 so the flatten path fits in a small buffer.

## Lane

flint (sim). Ember is filing, not claiming — though the scope is small enough that ember could ship it if flint hasn't picked it up by next autopilot cycle and the sim lane is otherwise quiet.

## Priority

P3 because it's strictly test-infra, but it's a load-bearing precondition for 6gf.3's reliability. Should ideally land before or concurrent with 6gf.1 (the recursive step), NOT after, so the reference is locked in before the refactor begins.

title: Verify AGENTS.md→CLAUDE.md symlink across Claude/codex/gemini + broader mirror inventory
description:
Verify the newly-landed `AGENTS.md` → `CLAUDE.md` symlink setup (mayor branch commit 5ff9e16) works end-to-end across every agent/tool surface that reads project instructions. Mayor filed this and the accompanying broader-mirror idea — **worker to execute, not mayor.**

## Background

Claude Code auto-loads `CLAUDE.md` but does NOT auto-load `AGENTS.md`. Previously the only project instruction file was named `AGENTS.md`, meaning its content (crew pool, mayor seat, afk mode, bd workflow, landing-the-plane) was invisible to every Claude session that didn't manually open it. Fix: `CLAUDE.md` is now the real file, `AGENTS.md` is a symlink to it. Cross-agent convention also satisfied (codex/gemini look for `AGENTS.md`).

## Scope — verification matrix

For each surface below, confirm the same content is loaded/visible:

- [ ] **Claude Code session startup** (expected: works — Claude auto-loads `CLAUDE.md`)
- [ ] **Trident review fan-out** (9-way Claude + Codex + Gemini). Each review agent spawned via `claude -p`, `codex exec`, and `gemini -m ... --yolo` with the project root as cwd. Does each resolve the symlinked `AGENTS.md` correctly? Do they see the same content Claude sees?
- [ ] **`codex exec --full-auto --skip-git-repo-check`** background sessions launched from the repo. Confirm codex picks up `AGENTS.md` (its convention) and that the symlink resolves.
- [ ] **`gemini -m gemini-3-pro-preview --yolo`** background sessions. Same check.
- [ ] **cmux / claude-in-chrome** sessions that walk the project tree for context.
- [ ] **Git operations on a fresh clone** (`git clone`, `git checkout`). Confirm the symlink is restored as a symlink, not as a regular file with literal content "CLAUDE.md". On macOS this Just Works; cross-platform verification is part of the bead if/when the project ever builds on Linux/Windows (currently macOS-only per environment notes).
- [ ] **`bd onboard`** reading project instructions.

## Broader scope (edward's extension)

Edward's stated intent: mayor (future) owns mirroring of other Claude-specific project assets to cross-agent paths *as long as* each mirror is compatible with Trident review and spawned-agent fan-out. Inventory candidates:

- `.claude/` skills, hooks, commands — are any of these useful to codex/gemini? Should they be exposed via a neutral path?
- `~/.claude/skills/*` user-global skills that this project relies on.
- Slash commands that land project policy (`/ship`, `/diagram`, `/loop`).

**Do NOT do the broader mirroring work yet.** Edward said "not yet." Use this bead as the parking spot for the inventory when he wants it cashed in.

## Acceptance

1. Every surface in the matrix confirmed loading the same bytes (or documented gap).
2. Inventory of Claude-specific assets that might benefit from cross-agent mirroring filed as a comment on this bead (separate from the verification, can come later).

## Parking

Parked low priority. Not urgent. Unblock when: edward wants this tested OR a cross-agent review run misreads project instructions and we need to diagnose.

Mayor does not own execution. A worker seat picks this up through `bd ready` when priorities allow, or edward explicitly assigns.

title: Implicit per-file locks across in-flight branches have no convention
description:
**Discovered by:** cairn (AFK poll scout) on 2026-04-11, extracted from the `.ship-notes/cairn-3au-claude-md-coordination-draft.md` draft that was superseded when 3au closed.

## Problem

When a crew agent ships a task on a feature branch that hasn't merged to main yet, the files it touches are implicitly locked at the git layer — but nothing in AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, or bd records which files a branch owns. A second agent can claim a ready task, build a plan, and only discover the overlap at code-review merge time.

## Live evidence (current session, 2026-04-11 poll)

- `flint/1v0.1-16bit-cells` @ 7fe36bc — 1v0.1 is CLOSED in bd but the branch is **not yet merged to main** (origin/main is 910d16c). Touches `src/octree/node.rs`, `src/octree/store.rs`, `src/octree/cell.rs`, `src/render/svdag*`, WGSL shaders.
- `flint/88d-store-compaction` @ 586a884 — 88d shipped but the branch is not yet merged to main. Touches `src/octree/store.rs`, `src/sim/world.rs`.

Both branches are invisible to `bd ready` — the tasks are closed, so bd thinks the files are free. Any cairn-shaped pickup touching `node.rs` / `store.rs` / `world.rs` in the next few hours (until edward merges the branches) would hit a merge conflict at push time.

## Why it's not solved by 3au's landed policy

3au closed after edward landed gate-tier rubric + peer-autonomy + afk mode across global CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md (commits 917e973, 910d16c). Those address:
- Plan-gate dashboard → partially (gate-tier rubric reduces what parks at gate)
- AFK protocol when blocked → fully (afk mode + global "When blocked: poll, don't stop")

But the landed policy does NOT address file-level lock discovery. An agent picking up work today still has to derive the lock map from:
1. `bd list --status in_progress` (captures claimed-but-not-shipped)
2. `git worktree list` + per-branch file inspection (captures shipped-but-not-merged)
3. `.ship-notes/plan-*.md` scan (captures drafted-plan-but-not-claimed)

All three sources must be checked together. Nothing automates this.

## Proposed convention (minimum viable)

Add to AGENTS.md under a new "File ownership" heading:

> When you claim a bd task, the files you intend to touch are implicitly locked by your branch until the branch merges to main. Before claiming, derive the current lock map from three sources:
> 1. `bd list --status in_progress` — active claims
> 2. `git worktree list` + recent commits per worktree — shipped-but-unmerged branches
> 3. `ls .ship-notes/plan-*.md` — drafted plans for tasks you might overlap
>
> The "files I'm touching" list belongs in the plan file header, not in your head.
>
> If you find overlap with an already-in-flight branch, pick a different task or coordinate the sequence via `bd comments add` on both task ids before claiming. Leave a note even if you decide to proceed — that's the discoverable audit trail.

## Stretch goal (not in scope for this bead)

- `bd file-locks` query helper that merges the three sources.
- Plan file header with a machine-readable `files-touched:` list that bd can cross-reference.
- Auto-annotation on `bd ready` output marking tasks whose scope overlaps an unmerged branch.

## See also

- `.ship-notes/cairn-3au-claude-md-coordination-draft.md` — original pattern 1 draft (now stale, 3au is closed; this bead is the extracted live-pattern).
- hash-thing-3au (closed 2026-04-11) — policy parent that resolved patterns 2+3 but not this one.

title: Mayor audit: re-tier bd ready under gate-tier rubric
description:
**Assignee: mayor**

Walk the current `bd ready` list and tag each task with its gate tier under the rubric in global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` → "Gate Tiers."

## Output

For each ready task, add a one-line bead comment:

> **Gate:** autonomous | design
> **Why:** <one line>

If design, flag the one-way-door thing that makes it design. If autonomous, note any design-adjacent concerns the worker should watch for.

## What 'autonomous' looks like here

- Refactors, bug fixes, test coverage, clippy, format.
- Implementing an already-approved algorithm (e.g., `hash-thing-3fq.1` multi-resolution procedural gen only if the shape is already agreed).
- Internal data-structure choices (e.g., container picks, iteration order).

## What 'design' looks like here

- New rules / new rendering modes / new controls.
- PRNG family (already decided but next-up terrain seed could reopen).
- Material system boundaries (`hash-thing-1v0.2` material registry — how materials are identified and referenced is design).
- Anything that changes what a user sees or can do.

## Rules

- **Re-tier, don't implement.** Mayor frames, workers build.
- When torn, one-way-door heuristic: expensive to reverse → design; cheap → autonomous.
- When mayor re-tiers a task as design, park it same as any design-gate task (blocked + comment).

Ship via /ship.
close_reason: Audit complete. 24 ready beads tagged with gate tier (autonomous/design/epic/mayor work) + one-line why. See per-bead audit comments.

title: probe(WorldCoord) sibling — distinguish 'is cell live' from 'is region realized'
description:
Surfaced by 819 trident review (evolutionary lens, both Claude + Codex). The 819 plan establishes that World::get(x,y,z) on out-of-bounds coordinates is a sanctioned pure read returning 0 (treating the unrealized world as empty). Reviewers proposed a probe() sibling that makes the intent explicit:

fn probe(&self, coord: WorldCoord) -> CellState

vs.

fn get(&self, coord: WorldCoord) -> CellState  // existing

## Why two methods?
Different callers want different semantics:

1. **Hashlife stepper** wants 'what is this cell currently' for a 3x3x3 neighborhood read. OOB-empty is correct (the world wraps the empty universe). Stepper calls probe().

2. **Render code** wants 'what's actually in storage' for visualization. Distinguishing 'unrealized' from 'realized empty' matters for debug overlays (e.g. 'render the realized region boundary in red'). Render calls get() and a separate is_realized().

3. **Future GC / pruning** wants 'is this region worth keeping' which is again different. GC walks from get() but skips probe() semantics.

Today get() conflates all three. probe() is the semantic alias that promises 'I am asking as a stepper, OOB is fine, never warns/asserts'.

## Why deferred from 819
The 819 plan absorbed this by dropping debug_assert! from get(), which is sufficient for the bug fix. probe() is the cleaner long-term split — file as follow-up so flint's stepper lane can adopt it without re-litigating the 819 contract.

## Lane
flint (sim/octree/Hashlife). The probe() consumer is the recursive Hashlife stepper.

## Blocked by
hash-thing-6gf.1 (Recursive Hashlife step) — the consumer must exist before the API is meaningful.

## See also
- .ship-notes/cairn-819-review-pack-2026-04-10T2335/synthesis-evolutionary.md
- .ship-notes/cairn-819-review-pack-2026-04-10T2335/_findings-running.md item under 'deferred to follow-ups'
- hash-thing-6gf.1
- hash-thing-7zc (WorldCoord newtype — paired)

title: WorldCoord(i64) / LocalCoord newtypes for coordinate-contract clarity
description:
Surfaced by 819 trident review (evolutionary lens — Codex called this the 'deepest insight' in synthesis). The current API is u64 for world coordinates, but the design tension is acute: 819 fixes 'OOB silently aliases' for u64 inputs, but 'the world is infinitely explorable in each direction' implies signed coordinates (u64::MAX is not the same shape as 'unbounded').

## Problem
Today we have one numeric type — u64 — for both:
1. Local cell index inside the realized root cube (0..side, where side fits in u64)
2. Global world position (could be -2^31..2^31 once recentering / signed coords land)

These are NOT the same. Mixing them creates exactly the bug class 819 fixed. Adding more u64 APIs without distinguishing them just sets up more 819-grade bugs.

## Proposal
Two newtypes:

#[derive(Copy, Clone, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Debug)]
struct WorldCoord(pub i64);  // signed, 'where in the infinite world'

#[derive(Copy, Clone, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Debug)]
struct LocalCoord(pub u64);  // unsigned, 'index inside the realized root'

Conversion is explicit:
- world_to_local(world: (WorldCoord, WorldCoord, WorldCoord), region: &RealizedRegion) -> Option<(LocalCoord, LocalCoord, LocalCoord)>
- local_to_world(local: (LocalCoord, LocalCoord, LocalCoord), region: &RealizedRegion) -> (WorldCoord, WorldCoord, WorldCoord)

## Why
- Compiler enforces 'don't mix global and local' instead of comments.
- ensure_contains takes WorldCoord and returns 'realized region grew' as a typed signal.
- get/set take WorldCoord — no more u64::MAX confusion.
- Spark's terrain layer naturally generates WorldCoord; SVDAG build naturally consumes LocalCoord.

## Why deferred from 819
Codex flagged this as the deepest tension but explicitly noted: this is e9h-scope or larger, not 819-scope. The 819 plan now marks ensure_contains(u64,u64,u64) as 'provisional pending coordinate-contract design', which is the placeholder for this ticket.

## Blocked by
- hash-thing-819 (defines the contract today as u64-based)
- hash-thing-e9h (implements the lazy expansion that needs to know about signed direction)

## See also
- .ship-notes/cairn-819-review-pack-2026-04-10T2335/synthesis-evolutionary.md
- .ship-notes/cairn-819-review-pack-2026-04-10T2335/_findings-running.md item #7
- hash-thing-e9h
- hash-thing-ica (RealizedRegion — paired)

title: RealizedRegion { origin, level } value type
description:
Surfaced by 819 trident review (evolutionary lens, both Claude + Codex). Today the World tracks (root: NodeId, level: u32) — the (0,0,0)..side cube is implicit. The reviewers proposed encapsulating this as a value type:

struct RealizedRegion {
    origin: (i64, i64, i64),  // anchor in world coords
    level: u32,                // size = 1 << level
}

## Why
- Once we go to signed coords (see WorldCoord newtype follow-up), the implicit (0,0,0) origin assumption breaks. The realized region might be centered elsewhere after recentering.
- Makes 'is this point in the realized region?' a method on the value, not scattered arithmetic in get/set/ensure.
- Render code can ask the World for a RealizedRegion to compute world extents instead of reading internal fields.
- Better debug print: 'realized: origin=(-1024,0,0) level=12 (4096^3)' beats 'side=4096'.

## Scope
- Add RealizedRegion struct in src/sim/world.rs (or a new src/sim/region.rs).
- contains(x,y,z) -> bool, side() -> u64, octant_of(point) -> u8.
- World stores a RealizedRegion instead of (root, level) — or has both, with the region as a derived view.
- Update renderer to read region instead of side().

## Why deferred from 819
Architectural cleanup that doesn't fix the bug — pure scope creep on a P1 fix. Filed now so the reviewers' insight isn't lost.

## Open question
Does this conflict with hash-thing-e9h's lazy root expansion? Probably not — e9h grows the root upward while keeping (0,0,0) as the corner. The RealizedRegion just encodes the result.

## See also
.ship-notes/cairn-819-review-pack-2026-04-10T2335/synthesis-evolutionary.md

title: ensure_region(aabb) primitive — wrap ensure_contains(point) as the special case
description:
Surfaced by 819 trident review (evolutionary lens, both Claude + Codex). Today World::ensure_contains(x,y,z) takes a single point. The trident reviewers independently proposed flipping the primitive: make ensure_region(min, max) the real worker, with ensure_contains(point) as a thin wrapper that calls ensure_region(point, point).

## Why
- Hashlife's natural batch op is 'realize this region', not 'realize this point'. A stepper that's about to walk a 3x3x3 neighborhood block wants a single ensure call covering the whole block, not 27 sequential point calls.
- Avoids re-walking the root expansion loop per cell when bulk-loading.
- Mirrors the SVDAG build path which already operates on regions.
- Cache-preservation contract is easier to phrase per-region than per-point.

## Scope
- Add World::ensure_region(min: (u64,u64,u64), max: (u64,u64,u64)) — grow root until both corners are in-bounds.
- Convert ensure_contains(x,y,z) to a wrapper.
- Update the SPEC.md commitments section to mention the region primitive.
- Tests: region wider than current root, region straddling current root, no-op when fully in-bounds.

## Blocked by
hash-thing-e9h must land first (the body of ensure_contains). After e9h ships, this becomes a refactor, not a new feature.

## Why deferred from 819
The 819 review pack rated this as 'strong evolutionary suggestion, defer to follow-up' — it's a good idea but scope creep on a P1 bug fix. Filed now so it isn't lost.

## See also
- .ship-notes/cairn-819-review-pack-2026-04-10T2335/synthesis-evolutionary.md (flywheel section)
- hash-thing-e9h

title: Cell::with_metadata panics on EMPTY — document or make total
description:
**Discovered by:** Codex (broad sweep) in the Trident review of d7c5b4d (hash-thing-1v0.1, 16-bit cells).

## Problem

`Cell::with_metadata(self, metadata)` is documented as "produce a new cell with the metadata replaced, material preserved." It looks like a total function, but it quietly delegates to `Cell::pack(self.material(), metadata)` — which now (post-1v0.1-fix) panics in both debug and release if `self.material() == 0` and `metadata != 0`.

That means:

```rust
let c = Cell::EMPTY.with_metadata(5); // panic!
```

No warning in the type system, no hint in the name. Easy trap: "I have a cell, I want to change its metadata" is a reasonable operation to assume is total.

## Fix options

1. **Documentation-only:** add a panic note to `with_metadata` spelling out the empty-cell case. Cheapest, matches the current documented contract of `pack`.
2. **Return `Option<Cell>`:** makes the partial function explicit in the type. Ripples to any caller in the future.
3. **No-op on empty:** `if self.is_empty() { Cell::EMPTY } else { Cell::pack(self.material(), metadata) }`. Hides the bug instead of exposing it — probably wrong.

Recommend option 1 for now. Revisit if we find callers in the wild that want to treat this as total.

## Scope

- `src/octree/node.rs:96-98` (`with_metadata`)
- Same treatment for `with_material` if it has a parallel case — check.

## Notes

P3 doc fix. Not currently exploitable (no caller uses `with_metadata` on `EMPTY`), but worth pinning before the metadata registry lands.

title: TraceResult.hit_material holds packed cell, not material id
description:
**Discovered during:** hash-thing-1v0.1 blocker fix work.

## Problem

`cpu_trace::TraceResult::hit_material: Option<u32>` and the SVDAG raycast loop return `child_slot & 0xFFFF` — the **raw packed cell word**, not the decoded material id.

This is a naming bug: the field name claims "material", but the value is `(material << 6) | metadata`. Every caller that wants the material id has to either know to `>> 6` or compare against the packed form. In the test suite, the expected value was updated from `Some(1)` to `Some(Cell::pack(1, 0).raw() as u32)` to paper over this — but the mismatch between the name and the content is a trap waiting for the next reader.

## Fix

Two options:
1. **Rename:** `hit_material` → `hit_cell` (or `hit_state`), update callers. Cheap, no behavior change.
2. **Decode at source:** keep the name, change the returned value to `(child_slot & 0xFFFF) >> METADATA_BITS`. Semantically truer to the name but loses information (metadata becomes unavailable downstream).

Option 1 is better — the renderer may eventually want metadata too (e.g., for shader-driven animation or state flags).

## Scope

- `src/render/svdag.rs:176` (`TraceResult`)
- `src/render/svdag.rs:308` (the assignment at the hit site)
- `src/render/svdag.rs:393-396` (test assertion + comment)
- Any real non-test caller (grep needed — none at filing time)

## Notes

Pure refactor. Could ride along with the SVDAG 31-bit overflow fix (hash-thing-x9r) since both touch the same hit-decoding area. Not a P1.

title: consider reducing trident review iterative pokes to cut costs
notes:
Full context: trident review fans out 9 parallel agents (3 models × 3 lenses). Observed costs/failure modes during 20260410-212112 run:

- 7/9 completed cleanly with strong, non-redundant signal
- Gemini standard + critical both stuck in gemini-3.1-pro-preview 429/RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED retry loop for ~15 min; had to TaskStop manually. Task-notification system never fired because the processes were still technically running.
- Even with 7 reviews we got strong consensus on 5 blockers + 10 importants + clean architectural thesis, so the marginal value of the 8th and 9th reviews is questionable for small codebases.
- Each review was ~10-30KB of structured findings, so synthesis cost scales sub-linearly (dedup + convergence).

Tradeoffs for reducing iterative pokes:
- Pro: lower token cost, less notification churn, cleaner synthesis handoff, fewer chances for stuck-agent failures
- Con: less visibility into which agents are stuck (though peeking output files works fine), harder to debug a degraded agent mid-run

Options:
- Collapse to dual/triple (Claude + Codex only) when the diff is small (<3000 LOC or <50 files changed)
- Keep 9-way for large diffs or architectural milestones
- Always kill stuck agents after N minutes past the fastest peer
- Consider making gemini agents best-effort (launch last, don't block synthesis if missing)

Decide after 2-3 more trident runs so we have fresh cost data.

title: Batch polish: SimpleRng seed-0 dead, node_count mismatch, octant 6 doc typo
description:
Code review nits/polish batch — all small, all worth landing before bigger refactors.

I9 — src/sim/world.rs:115-117 — SimpleRng::new(0) produces a dead xorshift64 (state 0 → state 0 forever). next_f64() returns 0.0 every call. Foot-gun for the deterministic-hash seed path (hash-thing-h34 epic) where hash(x,y,gen) can evaluate to 0.
FIX: Self(seed | 1) or feed through SplitMix64 before xorshift.

I10 — src/render/svdag.rs:51 — node_count = id_to_offset.len() counts visited ids (including leaves that are never emitted to the buffer). byte_size/36 == nodes.len()/9 == emitted interior count. Main prints both; they don't match.
FIX: node_count = nodes.len() / 9.

I11 — src/octree/node.rs:24 — doc comment says '6 = (-x, +y, +z) aka (0,1,0)' but octant_index(0,1,1) = 6. Zero runtime impact; high confusion cost for the next person debugging octant math against the doc.
FIX: (0,1,0) → (0,1,1).

Also pre-known but worth including: add debug_assert!(side.is_power_of_two()) at the top of Svdag::build and store.from_flat (part of I5 but trivial to batch here).

All three are mechanical and can go in one small ship.

title: AppImage wrapping for Linux release artifact (follow-up to xb7.3 bare binary)
description:
Scout finding (ember, autopilot dry-queue pass).

## Context

hash-thing-xb7.3 originally asked for 'Single-file Linux distribution via AppImage. Bare binary as fallback.' Ember shipped the bare-binary half as a `linux-bin` job in `.github/workflows/release.yml` (sibling to windows-exe from xb7.4), which publishes `hash-thing-linux-x86_64` to the GitHub release on tag pushes.

This bead tracks the remaining half: wrapping the same binary in an AppImage.

## Scope

- Download appimagetool (pin by SHA256 or tagged release; `continuous` is a floating tag and should be avoided per the same supply-chain reasoning that xb7.1 applied to GitHub Actions)
- AppDir skeleton: `AppDir/usr/bin/hash-thing`, `AppRun` symlink to the binary, `.desktop` file with Name/Exec/Type/Icon, and an icon (see 'Icon' below)
- Run appimagetool against AppDir → `hash-thing-linux-x86_64.AppImage`
- Add a second `gh release upload` step so both the bare binary AND the AppImage land as release assets

## Icon

No icon exists in the repo today. Options:
1. Generate a placeholder SVG inline in the workflow (single-character 'H' on a colored square) — ember-safe, no design call
2. File a small design bead for a real icon — edward's call
3. Ship with no icon — appimagetool accepts this with a warning, gives users a generic icon

Minimum viable is option 3 (no icon) or option 1 (placeholder). Whoever picks this up can decide; option 1 is the scout-friendly default.

## Why split from xb7.3

xb7.3's bare-binary half is pure cargo+gh infrastructure with zero new dependencies. Adding appimagetool requires supply-chain pinning of a third-party binary, placeholder icon generation, and .desktop file assembly — more moving parts, more to get wrong on first push, and no way to dry-run locally because AppImage tooling isn't installed on dev machines. Shipping the bare binary separately delivers the 80% use case (developers + CI consumers can grab a Linux binary from the release) while the AppImage work gets its own commit + review cycle.

## Lane

ember or cairn. Pure infra, no code-path changes.

Stale worktree .beads/ directory diverges silently from main repo bd state
description:
**Discovered by:** cairn (AFK poll, 2026-04-11) while running a routine unblock check from `.claude/worktrees/sparkling-baking-cookie/`.

## Problem

Each git worktree under `.claude/worktrees/` has its own `.beads/` directory, and `bd` auto-discovers from cwd. If a worktree is created, used briefly, then not touched for a while, its `.beads/` holds a stale snapshot while the main repo's `.beads/` keeps accumulating issues.

Concretely in this worktree (`sparkling-baking-cookie`):
- `.beads/issues.jsonl` — last written 2026-04-10 23:54 (~11h old)
- `.beads/beads.db` — **missing** (no dolt db file)
- `.beads/daemon.log` — missing
- Result: `bd ready` from cwd returns "No open issues" and `bd show hash-thing-819` errors with "no issue found matching" — completely divorced from reality.

Main repo's `.beads/`:
- `.beads/issues.jsonl` — current
- `.beads/beads.db` — 372KB, active
- `.beads/daemon.log` — 49KB, fresh

## Symptoms

1. Routine AFK polls silently report nothing to do, even when work is queued.
2. `bd create` from the stale worktree might write to the stale jsonl without syncing, creating drift that only shows up on the next manual import.
3. `bd show <id>` errors on valid ids, which an agent can misread as "the task was deleted" instead of "my local bd is broken".

## Workaround (temporary)

Run all `bd` commands with explicit cwd:

    cd /Users/edward/projects/hash-thing && BEADS_ACTOR=cairn bd <subcommand>

The trailing "Shell cwd was reset" confirms the subprocess cd doesn't leak to later commands, so this is safe per-invocation.

## Root cause (hypothesis)

1. `.claude/worktrees/` are created via `git worktree add`, which doesn't initialize bd.
2. On first use each worktree runs `bd init` or inherits a copy from the main repo, but doesn't hook into the main repo's daemon.
3. When the agent leaves and comes back after the daemon has moved on, the worktree's cached state is frozen.

Possible fixes (ordered by cost):
- **Documentation:** AGENTS.md onboarding explicitly says "always run bd from main repo path" or "always `bd import` at session start in a worktree."
- **Shim:** `.beads/` in a worktree becomes a symlink to the main repo's `.beads/` at worktree creation time. Single source of truth.
- **bd-aware worktrees:** `bd worktree register` subcommand that sets up daemon-shared state. Out of scope for this repo; would be an upstream feature request.

## Why P4

This is a footgun, not a blocker — once you know, the workaround is cheap. Logging it so the next afk cycle or new agent doesn't rediscover the same dead-reckoning failure.

## See also

- hash-thing-7ii (file-ownership convention) — sibling coordination-pain bead from the same poll.
- `cat .beads/actor` vs `echo $BEADS_ACTOR` — another divergence source: main repo's actor file may say `flint` while your worktree's says `cairn`, and bd picks up the cwd's actor. Cross-worktree `cd` can accidentally file beads under the wrong audit identity if BEADS_ACTOR isn't explicitly exported.

title: Background CLI tasks can hang in 429 retry loops with no task-notification
notes:
PARKED — logged but not acting on this right now. Capturing so the failure mode and proposed fix aren't lost.

## Problem

External CLI background tasks (gemini, codex, long-running npm/cargo) can get stuck in retry loops that never terminate and never fire a task-notification. The main thread, trusting the notification system, waits indefinitely.

## Live data point — 20260410-212112 trident code review

Ran 9-agent trident review. 7/9 landed cleanly with strong signal. Both Gemini standard + critical got stuck in a gemini-3.1-pro-preview RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED 429 retry loop for ~15 minutes. The processes were still technically running, so no task-notification ever fired. I didn't peek until the user explicitly asked 'possible to check on them?' The Gemini evolutionary agent (same CLI, same model flag) completed fine before capacity dried up, so the issue is specifically model-capacity-exhausted retry stalls, not CLI config.

Also noticed: '.ship-notes/' was in gemini CLI's default ignore patterns. Both Gemini agents tripped on reading prompt files before hitting 429. Harmless in this run because the prompts were inlined, but would block them on external context files in general.

## Proposed rule (for global CLAUDE.md when/if we decide to land it)

- Peek the output file within ~2 min of launch to confirm the process actually started (not crashed at argv parsing, not stuck on credential prompt).
- For multi-agent fan-outs: if any agent hasn't notified within ~3-5 min after the fastest peer completes, check its output file. If the tail shows repeated retries / 429 / capacity-exhausted / same error spinning — TaskStop it and proceed without it. Don't block synthesis on a stuck agent.
- Gemini CLI in particular: gemini-*-pro-preview is flaky on capacity; after 2 visible retries in the log, kill.
- Never equate 'no notification yet' with 'still making progress.' Always verify by reading the tail of the output file.

## Why parked

User wants this as a logged potential improvement, not a current rule. Not acting further right now. If this failure mode recurs in another session, land the rule above into ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md under a 'Background tasks' section.

## See also

hash-thing-l7d (reduce trident iterative pokes to cut costs) — related but distinct angle: that one is about cost reduction and collapsing 9→6 or 9→4 agents. This one is about stuck-task detection that applies regardless of agent count.
